proposals.title: Heterogeneity in the Cardiovascular Disease Effect Modifier: A Multi-Disease Meta-Analysis of PFAS-Associated Inflammatory and Metabolic Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies often treat cardiovascular disease (CVD) as a single, homogeneous modifier when assessing environmental exposures like PFAS, potentially obscuring disease-specific pathophysiological interactions. Given the distinct inflammatory and metabolic profiles of heart failure, coronary artery disease, and stroke, this aggregation limits mechanistic understanding and targeted risk assessment. Pre-2026 evidence suggests PFAS exposure alters key biomarkers relevant to these conditions, necessitating a disaggregated analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The association between serum PFAS levels and systemic inflammatory markers (e.g., CRP, IL-6) and bone/vascular metabolism markers (e.g., BGP, P1NP) is differentially modified by specific CVD subtypes (heart failure, coronary disease, stroke), rather than by a composite CVD status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and individual participant data (IPD) meta-analysis of pre-2026 studies reporting PFAS levels, inflammatory markers, and CVD status. Studies will be identified via bibliometric strategies akin to the researcher's prior work. Using multivariate mixed-effects models, we will test for interaction between PFAS quartiles and the three distinct CVD modifiers on biomarker levels, adjusting for age, sex, and treatment. Subgroup and meta-regression analyses will assess heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence that CVD subtypes are distinct effect modifiers for PFAS toxicity, moving beyond a monolithic CVD category. It will generate subtype-specific risk profiles linking PFAS to biomarker pathways, informing more precise public health guidelines and future clinical investigation into PFAS-related cardiovascular health.
proposals.novelty: While PFAS-CVD associations are known, this study innovatively disaggregates CVD into distinct pathophysiological modifiers for biomarker analysis, integrating the researcher's expertise in bibliometrics, acid/metabolic markers (e.g., zoledronic acid studies), and cardiovascular computational modeling trends.

proposals.title: A Bibliometric and Mechanistic Analysis of Biological Pathways Linking Physical Performance to Blood Pressure Regulation: Focus on Autonomic Function and Arterial Stiffness
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies consistently link physical performance to cardiovascular health, particularly blood pressure (BP) regulation, but the specific biological mediators remain inadequately synthesized. Understanding these pathways, such as autonomic nervous system function and arterial stiffness, is crucial for developing targeted interventions. A systematic, data-driven approach is needed to map the evolving research landscape and identify key mechanistic clusters for focused investigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between physical performance and BP is primarily mediated by distinct, interconnected biological pathways, with autonomic function and arterial stiffness representing central, high-impact hubs within the scientific literature and underlying physiology.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a comprehensive bibliometric analysis using the Web of Science and Scopus databases (pre-2026) to map the co-occurrence network of keywords and citations related to physical performance, BP, and candidate mediators. Tools like VOSviewer and CiteSpace will be employed for network visualization and cluster identification. Subsequently, we will perform a systematic review of the identified high-impact clusters to synthesize evidence on specific mechanisms, such as heart rate variability (autonomic function) and pulse wave velocity (arterial stiffness), and their biochemical correlates (e.g., inflammatory factors, endothelial function markers).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, quantitative map of the research field, identifying the most significant and emerging mechanistic links. It will synthesize disparate evidence into a coherent framework, highlighting priority pathways for future clinical and biochemical research, akin to the approach used in recent atherosclerosis CFD and macrophage bibliometric studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of computational bibliometric network analysis with a focused narrative review on specific biochemical and physiological mediators (e.g., inflammatory factors, vascular calcification markers), applying methods successful in other cardiovascular domains to the underexplored nexus of physical performance and BP mechanisms.

proposals.title: Differential Associations of Antihypertensive Drug Classes with Serum PFAS Levels and Cardiovascular Biomarkers: A Stratified Pharmaco-epidemiological Analysis
proposals.motivation: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are persistent environmental contaminants linked to adverse cardiovascular health outcomes, including hypertension. While antihypertensive treatment is a key clinical intervention, it is unknown whether specific drug classes (e.g., ACE inhibitors, ARBs, diuretics, calcium channel blockers) modify the association between PFAS exposure and cardiovascular risk biomarkers. Understanding these potential drug-environment interactions is critical for personalized risk assessment and treatment strategies in exposed populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength and direction of the association between serum PFAS levels and key cardiovascular inflammatory/profibrotic biomarkers (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α, MMP-9) will differ significantly when analyses are stratified by antihypertensive medication class, with certain classes potentially attenuating the pro-inflammatory effects of PFAS.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of existing cohort data (e.g., NHANES cycles up to 2018-2020) linking serum PFAS levels, medication use, and serum biomarkers. Participants on antihypertensive monotherapy will be stratified into drug class groups. Using multivariable linear regression models adjusted for confounders, we will test for interaction between PFAS quartiles and drug class on biomarker levels. Significance of interaction terms and stratified effect estimates will be the primary analytical focus, employing methods akin to pharmaco-epidemiological studies of drug-outcome interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first pharmaco-epidemiological evidence on whether antihypertensive drug classes differentially modify the cardiovascular biomarker response to PFAS exposure. It will inform clinical guidelines by identifying if specific medications might be preferable for hypertensive patients with high PFAS burden and contribute mechanistic insights into drug-environment interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has linked PFAS to cardiovascular risk and studied antihypertensives broadly, the novel stratification by specific drug class to examine effect modification on biomarker pathways represents a significant advance beyond existing ecological or non-stratified analyses.

proposals.title: The Prognostic Value of Concurrent Changes in Bone Turnover Markers and Blood Pressure for Cardiovascular Mortality: A Longitudinal Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research indicates that bone metabolism and cardiovascular health are pathophysiologically linked, with conditions like atherosclerosis and vascular calcification sharing common pathways. Clinical studies, including those on treatments like zoledronic acid, often measure changes in bone turnover markers (e.g., BGP, P1NP) and blood pressure (BP) separately, but the clinical significance of their concurrent change is unknown. Determining whether these combined biochemical and hemodynamic shifts predict hard endpoints like mortality would provide a novel, integrative risk assessment tool.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients exhibiting significant concurrent improvements in both bone turnover marker profiles (specifically reductions in B-CTx and increases in P1NP/BGP) and systolic blood pressure will have a significantly lower risk of cardiovascular mortality and major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) compared to those with discordant or unfavorable changes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective longitudinal analysis of a large clinical cohort (e.g., from the UK Biobank or a treated osteoporosis cohort) with serial measurements of bone markers (B-CTx, P1NP, BGP) and BP. Using time-varying Cox proportional hazards models, we will assess the association between pre-defined categories of concurrent change (e.g., 'favorable both', 'unfavorable both', 'discordant') and the primary outcome of cardiovascular mortality. Secondary analyses will use machine learning techniques, specifically a Random Survival Forest model, to identify non-linear interactions and rank the predictive importance of the rate and magnitude of change in each marker alongside traditional risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence on whether combined monitoring of bone and vascular parameters offers superior prognostic value. It will translate common clinical laboratory and vital sign data into a practical, integrative risk stratification framework. The findings could inform guidelines for the concurrent management of osteoporosis and hypertension, particularly in aging populations.
proposals.novelty: While the bone-vascular axis is recognized, no pre-2026 study has explicitly tested the prognostic utility of *concurrent longitudinal changes* in specific bone turnover markers and blood pressure. The application of survival machine learning models to this specific biochemical-physiological interaction is also novel, moving beyond simple correlation to predictive modeling.

proposals.title: Functional Data Analysis of Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Cognitive Performance Trajectories: A Study of Co-Evolution in Aging and Cardiovascular Risk
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal studies of blood pressure (BP) and cognitive performance typically analyze discrete time points, losing the rich temporal dynamics of their evolution. This is a critical gap, as understanding the continuous, time-varying relationship between vascular health (BP) and brain function is central to aging and cardiovascular disease research, including atherosclerosis. Pre-2026, functional data analysis (FDA) offers powerful tools to model such trajectories but has been underutilized in this specific co-evolutionary context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional trajectories of systolic and diastolic blood pressure are significantly associated with the functional trajectories of specific cognitive domain performances over time, and that this association is modified by inflammatory biomarker levels (e.g., serum inflammatory factors) and vascular health status.
proposals.method: We will apply FDA to treat repeated measures of BP (systolic, diastolic) and neuropsychological test scores as smooth curves over time, using B-spline basis functions and penalized smoothing for curve estimation. The core analysis will employ functional linear models (FLMs) and functional principal component analysis (FPCA) to model the relationship between the BP curves and cognitive performance curves. We will extend this to a functional concurrent model to assess time-lagged influences. Covariates will include baseline PFAS levels, bone turnover markers (e.g., BGP, P1NP), and treatment history, aligning with the researcher's thematic expertise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic characterization of the BP-cognition relationship, moving beyond static associations to identify critical temporal windows of influence. It will contribute a methodological framework for analyzing co-evolving physiological and functional trajectories in longitudinal health studies, with direct relevance for understanding the progression of cognitive decline linked to vascular aging.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated application of FDA to model the *co-evolution* of BP and cognition as continuous processes, and in testing how this relationship is moderated by biochemical markers of inflammation and bone metabolism, a synthesis not previously explored in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Bibliometric and Computational Fluid Dynamics Analysis of Hemodynamic and Inflammatory Factors Associated with Blood Pressure Stability in Aging: A Focus on PFAS Exposure and Vascular Resilience
proposals.motivation: Most cardiovascular research focuses on factors causing blood pressure (BP) change, particularly hypertension. However, a significant subset of the aging population maintains stable BP despite physiological decline, representing a critical but understudied model of vascular resilience. Understanding the biological and environmental factors, such as exposure to per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), that maintain this stability could reveal novel protective mechanisms and therapeutic targets, shifting the paradigm from disease treatment to resilience promotion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stable BP in aging is associated with a distinct profile of inflammatory biomarkers, bone-vascular metabolic cross-talk, and favorable hemodynamic flow patterns, which may be significantly modulated by environmental factors like PFAS levels.
proposals.method: We will conduct a dual-method analysis. First, a bibliometric study using VOSviewer or CiteSpace will map the historical and emerging research landscape linking inflammatory factors (e.g., serum IFCS), bone markers (BGP, P1NP, B-CTx), PFAS, and vascular health to identify key knowledge clusters and gaps. Second, we will apply pre-2026 computational fluid dynamics (CFD) models, using finite-element methods in ANSYS Fluent or OpenFOAM, to simulate arterial hemodynamics in idealized and patient-specific geometries derived from public datasets, analyzing wall shear stress stability under varying conditions of vascular stiffness linked to the identified biochemical markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrative analysis framework combining bibliometric trends with mechanistic CFD modeling to define the 'stable BP phenotype.' It will generate a novel hypothesis set on the interplay between environmental exposures (PFAS), systemic inflammation, bone metabolism, and hemodynamic forces in maintaining vascular homeostasis, directly informing future longitudinal clinical studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the investigative focus from BP change to BP stability, and in the unique methodological combination of bibliometric analysis of clinical/biochemical factors with engineering-based CFD simulations of hemodynamic stability, a synthesis not previously applied to this question.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Evaluating the Simultaneous Impact of Hydrogen-Rich Water on Serum Uric Acid Levels, Hypertension, and Hepatic Steatosis in a Hyperuricemic Cohort
proposals.motivation: Elevated serum uric acid (SUA) is a significant risk factor for gout and is increasingly linked to cardiometabolic comorbidities like hypertension and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD). Hydrogen-rich water (HRW), a novel antioxidant intervention, has shown promise in preliminary studies for reducing oxidative stress and improving metabolic parameters. However, its potential for simultaneously modulating SUA and these linked conditions remains underexplored, representing a gap in understanding its systemic therapeutic effects.
proposals.hypothesis: Daily supplementation with HRW will significantly reduce serum uric acid levels and, concurrently, improve markers of blood pressure control and hepatic lipid metabolism in individuals with hyperuricemia, compared to a placebo control.
proposals.method: We propose a 12-week, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial. 120 participants with hyperuricemia (SUA > 7.0 mg/dL) and at least one comorbidity (pre-hypertension or NAFLD) will be randomized to receive HRW or placebo water. Primary outcomes are changes in SUA, systolic/diastolic blood pressure (via 24-hour ambulatory monitoring), and hepatic steatosis index (via controlled attenuation parameter from FibroScan). Secondary outcomes include serum inflammatory factors (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α), lipid profiles, and liver enzymes. Statistical analysis will employ repeated-measures ANOVA to assess between-group differences over time, with post-hoc tests for specific time points, and mediation analysis to explore relationships between SUA reduction and improvements in other parameters.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level clinical evidence on the efficacy of HRW as a multi-target intervention for hyperuricemia and its common comorbidities. It will elucidate potential mechanistic links by correlating changes in SUA with inflammatory and metabolic markers. The findings could position HRW as a novel, non-pharmacological adjunctive strategy for managing interconnected metabolic syndrome components.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has examined HRW's effects on isolated conditions, this is the first controlled trial designed explicitly to test its simultaneous impact on the triad of hyperuricemia, hypertension, and hepatic steatosis, integrating clinical phenotyping with mechanistic serum biomarker analysis to assess systemic benefit.

proposals.title: A Bibliometric and Clinical Data Analysis of Long-Term Functional Outcomes Following High-Protein Nutritional Support in Critical Illness: A Focus on Muscle Strength and Inflammatory Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: While high-protein nutritional support in critical care aims to mitigate catabolism and improve 28-day survival, its long-term impact on physical function and muscle strength remains poorly characterized. This gap is significant, as survivors often face severe disability, impacting quality of life and healthcare costs. Pre-2026 research, including studies on inflammatory factors like serum IFcs in bone treatment, suggests biochemical markers may predict long-term outcomes, but this link is unexplored in critical care nutrition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-protein intervention in critically ill patients is associated with significantly better long-term (6-12 month) physical function and muscle strength, and that these functional outcomes correlate with specific early trajectories of inflammatory biomarkers (e.g., IL-6, CRP) and muscle turnover markers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-phase study. First, a bibliometric analysis (using tools like CiteSpace or VOSviewer) will map the existing research landscape on protein supplementation and long-term outcomes, identifying key knowledge gaps and emerging trends, similar to our prior work on macrophages in ischemia and CFD in atherosclerosis. Second, a retrospective cohort analysis will be performed on existing critical care datasets, employing multivariate regression and mixed-effects models to analyze the relationship between protein intake, serial biomarker levels (inflammatory and muscle-related), and long-term functional measures (e.g., 6-minute walk test, handgrip strength).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational map of research trends in post-ICU recovery nutrition and deliver novel empirical evidence linking early nutritional strategy to long-term disability. It will propose a predictive model integrating biochemical and clinical data to identify patients at risk for poor functional recovery, shifting the focus beyond short-term mortality.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the combined bibliometric and clinical data approach to a neglected endpoint (long-term function) and the explicit integration of inflammatory biomarker trajectories—a method informed by bone treatment research—into the analysis of nutritional intervention effectiveness.

proposals.title: Identification of Non-Responder Phenotypes to High-Protein Nutritional Intervention in Cardiometabolic Disease: A Data-Driven Inverse Problem Approach
proposals.motivation: High-protein diets are widely recommended for weight management and metabolic health, yet clinical responses are highly heterogeneous. Pre-2026 evidence suggests that in some patients, particularly those with subclinical renal stress or specific inflammatory states, high protein intake may not confer benefits and could even elevate risks, such as increased acid load or uremic toxin production. This inverse problem—identifying who does *not* benefit—is crucial for moving from one-size-fits-all guidelines to precision nutrition, thereby preventing iatrogenic harm and optimizing resource allocation in dietary interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct clinical and biochemical phenotypes, characterized by specific patterns in renal function markers, inflammatory cytokines, and acid-base balance parameters, can be identified from patient data and are predictive of a lack of cardiometabolic benefit from a high-protein dietary intervention.
proposals.method: We will apply an unsupervised-supervised hybrid pipeline to existing clinical trial or cohort data (e.g., involving PFAS exposure, bone turnover markers like BGP, B-CTx, P1NP, and inflammatory factors). First, we will use clustering algorithms (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models or hierarchical clustering) on baseline multi-omics and clinical data to identify potential phenotypes. Subsequently, we will train interpretable machine learning models (e.g., Regularized Logistic Regression, Random Forests, or XGBoost) to predict non-response (defined by changes in key outcomes like HOMA-IR, inflammatory markers, or body composition) from baseline features, validating the phenotypic clusters. Feature importance analysis will elucidate key discriminators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven framework for stratifying patients based on their likely response to high-protein diets, directly addressing the inverse problem of benefit prediction. It will contribute specific phenotypic profiles to the precision nutrition literature and offer a methodological template—combining bibliometric insight with clinical data analysis—applicable to other nutritional interventions. The findings could inform the design of future trials and clinical screening tools.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on identifying responders, this proposal explicitly targets the inverse problem of non-responders, a critical but understudied angle for safety and efficacy. It uniquely integrates Jun Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis, inflammatory markers (e.g., from PFAS/cytokine work), and bone/acid-base metabolism (from zoledronic acid studies) to define novel composite phenotypes beyond single biomarkers.

proposals.title: Long-term Efficacy and Safety of Zoledronic Acid and Denosumab Combination Therapy in Osteoporosis: A 5-Year Follow-up Study on Fracture Incidence and Sustained Biomarker Modulation
proposals.motivation: Combination anti-resorptive therapy with zoledronic acid and denosumab is a promising strategy for severe osteoporosis, but its long-term efficacy and safety profile beyond initial biomarker changes are poorly characterized. Given the chronic nature of osteoporosis and the established risks of long-term antiresorptive use, such as atypical femoral fractures and osteonecrosis of the jaw, a rigorous assessment of long-term outcomes is critically needed to inform clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that long-term (5-year) combination therapy will demonstrate superior sustained reduction in fracture rates compared to monotherapy, and that this clinical benefit will correlate with persistent suppression of bone turnover markers (B-CTx, P1NP) and inflammatory factors (serum IFcs), without an unacceptable increase in adverse event incidence.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, randomized, open-label, controlled extension study of the original cohort. Participants will be followed for 5 years, with primary endpoints being radiographically confirmed new fragility fractures and serious adverse events. Secondary endpoints include annual measurements of serum biomarkers (B-CTx, P1NP, BGP, IFcs). Statistical analysis will employ Kaplan-Meier survival analysis for fracture-free survival, Cox proportional hazards models to adjust for covariates, and repeated-measures ANOVA to analyze longitudinal biomarker trajectories. Safety will be assessed via systematic adverse event reporting and adjudication.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on the long-term fracture prevention benefit and safety of this specific combination regimen. It will establish whether the initial synergistic biochemical effects translate into durable clinical outcomes, directly informing treatment guidelines for high-risk osteoporosis patients.
proposals.novelty: While short-term biomarker studies exist, this proposal uniquely integrates long-term clinical fracture data with sustained biochemical profiling to holistically evaluate the risk-benefit ratio of combination therapy, moving beyond the proof-of-concept phase into practical, long-term management.

proposals.title: Correlating Continuous Wearable-Derived Physical Activity Metrics with Bone Mineral Density and Serum Inflammatory Biomarkers in Patients Receiving Anti-Resorptive Therapy: A Longitudinal Digital Health Study
proposals.motivation: The long-term management of osteoporosis and related bone metabolic diseases relies on periodic bone mineral density (BMD) scans and serum biomarker assays, which provide sparse, discontinuous snapshots of patient status. Pre-2026, the proliferation of consumer-grade wearables offers an unprecedented opportunity for continuous, real-world physical activity monitoring. However, robust correlations between high-resolution activity patterns (intensity, frequency, type) and clinically relevant outcomes like BMD changes and inflammatory marker levels (e.g., serum IFcs, BGP, B-CTx) remain underexplored, especially in patients undergoing specific treatments like zoledronic acid or denosumab.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable patterns of physical activity (e.g., daily moderate-vigorous activity minutes, impact load, activity variability) captured by wrist-worn accelerometers will show significant longitudinal correlations with changes in BMD and serum levels of bone turnover markers (P1NP, B-CTx) and inflammatory factors (IFcs) in patients on anti-resorptive therapy, providing a digital biomarker for treatment response.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month longitudinal observational study enrolling patients initiating zoledronic acid or denosumab therapy. Participants will wear a research-grade accelerometer (e.g., ActiGraph) continuously. Raw tri-axial acceleration data will be processed using validated algorithms (e.g., GGIR open-source package) to extract activity intensity, sedentary bouts, and metrics of mechanical loading. These time-series data will be aggregated into weekly features. Correlations and multivariate linear mixed-effects models will assess relationships between these digital activity features and serially measured serum biomarkers (BGP, P1NP, B-CTx, IFcs) and 6/12-month BMD changes, controlling for covariates like age and baseline health.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a novel framework for integrating continuous digital phenotyping with traditional biochemical and imaging endpoints in bone health. It will identify specific, actionable physical activity signatures associated with positive treatment response, potentially informing personalized activity prescriptions. The work will contribute a methodological blueprint for using wearables in chronic disease management beyond osteoporosis.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies link general exercise to bone health, this proposal uniquely leverages high-frequency, real-world wearable data as a continuous variable to model dynamic associations with a comprehensive panel of bone and inflammatory biomarkers in a defined clinical treatment cohort, moving beyond simple step counts to mechanistically relevant activity patterns.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Causal Nexus: A Mediation and Interaction Analysis of Sleep-Related Disorders, Hypertension, and Cardiovascular Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: Sleep-related disorders (SRD) and hypertension (HTN) are both established, potent risk factors for cardiovascular disease (CVD), the leading cause of global mortality. However, their clinical management is often siloed, and the pathophysiological interplay between SRD and HTN remains incompletely characterized. Determining whether they act as independent, additive, or synergistic drivers of CVD is critical for developing targeted, multi-system prevention strategies and optimizing public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SRD and HTN are not fully independent risk factors for CVD; instead, a significant portion of the CVD risk attributed to SRD is mediated through the development and severity of HTN, while a residual direct effect of SRD on CVD persists.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using large-scale electronic health record data (e.g., UK Biobank, pre-2026 releases). The primary analysis will employ causal mediation modeling, treating HTN as a potential mediator on the pathway from SRD (exposure) to incident CVD (outcome). We will use the R package 'mediation' to estimate natural direct and indirect effects, adjusting for confounders (age, sex, BMI, diabetes, lipid levels). Interaction on an additive scale will be assessed using relative excess risk due to interaction (RERI) to test for synergy between SRD and HTN.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative estimate of the proportion of SRD-associated CVD risk mediated by HTN, clarifying the primary pathophysiological link. It will establish whether SRD and HTN confer additive or supra-additive CVD risk, informing clinical guidelines on combined risk assessment. The findings will prioritize intervention targets, suggesting whether aggressive HTN control alone suffices in SRD patients or if direct SRD treatment is independently necessary for CVD risk reduction.
proposals.novelty: While SRD and HTN are linked, applying formal causal mediation analysis to large-scale clinical data to partition their effects on CVD is novel. This moves beyond establishing association to modeling the mechanistic pathway, a crucial step not yet widely undertaken in pre-2026 epidemiological studies of this triad.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Composite: A Bibliometric and Meta-Analytic Study of Risk Factor Profiles for Specific Cardiovascular Disease Endpoints
proposals.motivation: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is predominantly studied and reported as a composite, homogeneous endpoint in clinical trials and epidemiological research. This aggregation obscures potentially distinct etiologies and risk profiles for specific conditions like myocardial infarction, stroke, and heart failure, limiting the precision of prevention and treatment strategies. A systematic analysis is needed to challenge this paradigm and quantify the heterogeneity within the composite CVD outcome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established and emerging risk factors (e.g., PFAS exposure, inflammatory markers) exhibit significantly different magnitudes of association with specific CVD endpoints (e.g., stroke vs. heart failure) compared to the composite CVD outcome. The composite endpoint masks these critical differences.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-phase study. First, a bibliometric analysis (using VOSviewer or CiteSpace) will map the historical and current research landscape, visualizing the conceptual separation or conflation of specific CVD endpoints in the literature. Second, we will perform a focused meta-analysis of pre-2026 cohort studies and clinical trials that report both composite and specific endpoint data. Using random-effects models, we will calculate and compare pooled risk ratios (e.g., hazard ratios) for key exposures (prioritizing PFAS and inflammatory markers like serum IFcs) between the composite endpoint and its individual components, testing for heterogeneity using Cochran's Q and I² statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide quantitative evidence against the treatment of CVD as a single entity, offering a nuanced risk profile for specific diseases. It will inform the design of future studies and public health guidelines by advocating for endpoint-specific analysis. The integration of bibliometric trends with meta-analytic evidence will offer a unique, dual perspective on a fundamental methodological issue.
proposals.novelty: While meta-analyses often focus on a single endpoint, this proposal uniquely applies bibliometric techniques from the researcher's portfolio to first map the conceptual problem, then directly tests the statistical validity of the composite endpoint by decomposing it in a comparative meta-analysis of pre-existing data.

proposals.title: A Prospective Cohort Study on the Interaction of Sleep-Related Disorders and Hypertension in Predicting Incident Cardiovascular Disease: A Focus on Inflammatory and Metabolic Mediators
proposals.motivation: Sleep-related disorders (SRDs) like obstructive sleep apnea and hypertension (HTN) are established, often co-occurring risk factors for cardiovascular disease (CVD). However, pre-2026 evidence lacks prospective, longitudinal data to establish the temporal causality of their *interaction* on CVD incidence, and the mediating biochemical pathways (e.g., inflammation, bone/vascular cross-talk) remain underexplored. This gap limits targeted prevention strategies for this high-risk patient group.
proposals.hypothesis: The co-occurrence of SRD and HTN has a synergistic effect, significantly increasing the risk of incident CVD (myocardial infarction, stroke, heart failure) compared to either condition alone, and this risk is mediated by longitudinal changes in specific serum inflammatory factors (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α), vascular calcification markers (e.g., Bone Gla Protein), and bone turnover markers (e.g., P1NP, β-CTX).
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective cohort of 3000 adults, stratified into four groups: SRD-only, HTN-only, SRD+HTN, and controls. Participants will undergo baseline and annual polysomnography, 24-hour blood pressure monitoring, and serum collection for 5 years. CVD events will be adjudicated. We will employ Cox proportional hazards models to assess the interaction term (SRD*HTN) on CVD incidence. Mediation analysis will test the role of longitudinal changes in serum inflammatory factors (IL-6, TNF-α), Bone Gla Protein, P1NP, and β-CTX, measured via multiplex immunoassays, in explaining the observed risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for a causal, synergistic link between SRD and HTN on CVD, moving beyond associative studies. It will identify key biochemical mediators (inflammatory and bone-related markers) of this interaction, offering novel targets for mechanistic research and personalized risk stratification. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines for integrated SRD and HTN management to prevent CVD.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates longitudinal clinical phenotyping with specific biochemical pathways informed by the researcher's prior work on inflammatory factors and bone-vascular axis markers, to dissect the mechanistic interaction between SRD and HTN, a non-obvious approach beyond standard epidemiological risk factor analysis.

proposals.title: Mediating Pathways of Synergistic Cardiovascular Risk: A Multi-Omics and Bibliometric Analysis of Sleep-Related Disorders and Hypertension
proposals.motivation: Sleep-related disorders (SRD) and hypertension (HTN) are major, often co-occurring risk factors for cardiovascular disease (CVD), with evidence suggesting their combined effect is synergistic. However, the specific biological pathways—inflammatory, autonomic, or metabolic—that mediate this interaction remain poorly defined. Elucidating these mediators is critical for developing targeted interventions to mitigate the amplified CVD risk in this large patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the synergistic effect of SRD and HTN on CVD risk is primarily mediated by a dysregulated inflammatory axis, characterized by specific cytokine profiles and macrophage activation states, which can be identified through integrated multi-omics analysis.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-phase approach. First, a systematic bibliometric analysis (using VOSviewer or CiteSpace) will map the existing knowledge landscape of SRD, HTN, and CVD pathways, identifying key mechanistic clusters and research gaps. Second, we will analyze a clinical cohort with SRD, HTN, both, or neither, measuring serum levels of inflammatory factors (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α), autonomic markers (e.g., heart rate variability), and metabolic markers (e.g., PFAS as environmental metabolic disruptors, bone turnover markers like BGP and P1NP as vascular calcification proxies). Mediation analysis will be performed using structural equation modeling to quantify the contribution of each pathway to CVD risk indices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a mechanistic framework for the SRD-HTN interaction, identifying primary mediating pathways. It will generate a novel, integrated biomarker panel predictive of synergistic CVD risk. The findings will inform the design of future clinical trials testing pathway-specific treatments (e.g., anti-inflammatory agents) in comorbid patients.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely combines computational bibliometric trend analysis with targeted wet-lab biomarker validation to deconvolve a complex clinical synergy. It innovatively applies Jun Ma's expertise in bibliometrics and biochemical pathway analysis (e.g., from PFAS and zoledronic acid studies) to the under-explored intersection of sleep medicine and cardiometabolic disease.

proposals.title: Detecting High-Dimensional Sleep-Hypertension Interactions for Cardiovascular Risk Stratification: A Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Sleep disorders are a well-established, modifiable risk factor for hypertension and cardiovascular disease (CVD), but the complex, non-linear interactions between multidimensional sleep parameters (e.g., duration, efficiency, architecture, apnea-hypopnea index) and continuous hypertension metrics (e.g., 24-hour blood pressure variability, nocturnal dipping) remain poorly characterized. Traditional statistical methods are limited in detecting these high-order interactions, creating a knowledge gap in precision sleep medicine for CVD prevention. This study aims to bridge this gap using advanced machine learning techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning models will identify specific, high-dimensional interaction patterns between polysomnographic and actigraphy-derived sleep parameters and ambulatory blood pressure metrics that are significantly more predictive of subclinical cardiovascular dysfunction and future CVD risk than conventional linear models or individual parameters alone.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-dimensional datasets containing polysomnography, actigraphy, and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring from cohorts like the Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis (MESA). We will employ tree-based ensemble methods (e.g., Gradient Boosting Machines, Random Forests) specifically for their inherent ability to model interactions. To explicitly detect and interpret these interactions, we will implement techniques like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) interaction values and use of dedicated interaction detection algorithms within these ensembles. Model performance will be validated against metrics of arterial stiffness (pulse-wave velocity) and left ventricular mass index.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven map of critical sleep-hypertension interactions, moving beyond univariate associations. It will deliver a validated ML framework for interaction detection applicable to other complex physiological systems. The findings could inform the development of personalized, multi-parameter sleep intervention targets to mitigate hypertension and CVD risk.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to predict sleep apnea or hypertension separately, its focused use for explicit, high-dimensional interaction detection between continuous, multi-modal sleep and blood pressure data for CVD risk stratification is novel. This approach integrates Dr. Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis of cardiovascular themes and biochemical interaction studies into a cutting-edge computational methodology.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Risk Score for Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease Using Longitudinal EMR Data: Integrating Sleep Patterns and Blood Pressure Variability
proposals.motivation: Sleep disorders and hypertension are well-established, interconnected risk factors for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD), a leading cause of global mortality. Current risk assessment tools, like the pooled cohort equations, rely on static, cross-sectional measurements, failing to capture the dynamic interplay between these fluctuating physiological parameters over time. Leveraging the rich, longitudinal data within Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) offers an unprecedented opportunity to model these temporal relationships for more precise, personalized risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model trained on longitudinal EMR-derived sleep and blood pressure data will generate a dynamic risk score that predicts incident ASCVD events (e.g., myocardial infarction, stroke) with significantly greater accuracy than static, guideline-recommended risk scores.
proposals.method: We will extract longitudinal sleep disorder diagnoses, medication codes, and serial blood pressure measurements from a large-scale EMR database (e.g., MIMIC-IV or a comparable institutional repository). Time-series features, including variability indices, trends, and cross-correlations between sleep and BP data, will be engineered. A recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture, specifically a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, will be trained to process these sequential inputs and output a continuously updated risk probability. Model performance will be validated against a held-out test set and benchmarked against the ASCVD risk score using area-under-the-curve (AUC) metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, data-driven framework for real-time, individualized ASCVD risk assessment that directly incorporates temporal physiological patterns. It will provide a proof-of-concept for moving beyond static risk calculators towards dynamic clinical decision support tools. The findings will also offer new insights into the temporal dynamics linking sleep health and cardiovascular pathophysiology.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have linked sleep and BP to CVD, this proposal uniquely focuses on modeling their *joint longitudinal trajectories* from real-world EMR data using deep learning for continuous risk updating, a significant advance over cross-sectional or single-domain analyses. It synergistically combines Jun Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis of atherosclerosis and the study of biochemical markers (e.g., inflammatory factors) with a novel computational fluid dynamics-inspired approach to modeling physiological 'flows' over time.

proposals.title: Global Burden of Cardiovascular Disease Attributable to the Co-Occurrence of Sleep-Related Disorders and Hypertension: A Population-Attributable Fraction Analysis
proposals.motivation: Sleep-related disorders (SRD), like obstructive sleep apnea, and hypertension (HTN) are major, interconnected risk factors for cardiovascular disease (CVD), the leading cause of global mortality. While their individual contributions are recognized, the global CVD burden specifically attributable to their co-occurrence is not quantified. Estimating this joint population-attributable fraction (PAF) is critical for prioritizing integrated public health interventions and resource allocation, especially given the rising global prevalence of both conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The synergistic interaction between SRD and HTN accounts for a significant proportion of the global CVD burden, and this attributable fraction varies substantially by region, age, and sex due to differences in risk factor prevalence and underlying population health.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comparative risk assessment analysis using the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) Study framework (pre-2026 methodology). We will first perform a systematic review and meta-analysis to derive pooled relative risk estimates for CVD (ischemic heart disease, stroke) associated with the co-occurrence of SRD and HTN. Using GBD 2019 data inputs (pre-2026), we will obtain global, regional, and national prevalence estimates for SRD and HTN. The PAF will be calculated using established formulas, accounting for risk factor interdependence. Statistical uncertainty will be propagated using Monte Carlo simulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first global, regional, and national estimates of CVD burden attributable to the co-exposure of SRD and HTN. It will highlight the public health impact of this comorbidity, offering evidence to guide integrated screening and management strategies. The results will also identify high-burden populations for targeted policy action.
proposals.novelty: While prior GBD analyses estimate burdens for individual risk factors, this proposal innovatively applies the PAF methodology to quantify the burden from the specific *interaction* of two highly prevalent, biologically linked conditions, moving beyond additive models to assess their synergistic impact on global CVD.

proposals.title: Causal Disentanglement of Hypoxic Duration in Cortical Injury: A Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation Approach to Isolate Treatment Effect from Concurrent Physiological Confounders
proposals.motivation: In critical care, hypoxic episodes are complex, multi-factorial events where duration is conflated with insult severity, making its independent contribution to neurological injury unclear. Pre-2026 observational studies show strong correlations but cannot establish causality, hindering the development of targeted neuroprotective strategies. A method to isolate the specific effect of duration is needed to inform clinical decision-making and prognostication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the duration of a hypoxic episode has a significant, independent causal effect on the extent of cortical injury, even after rigorously controlling for confounding physiological variables such as hypoxia depth, hypotension burden, and patient premorbid status.
proposals.method: We will implement a prospective observational cohort study with continuous multimodal monitoring (EEG, NIRS, arterial line) in a neurocritical care unit. Using pre-2026 causal inference frameworks, we will apply Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE) to model hypoxic duration as the 'treatment' variable. The Super Learner ensemble algorithm will be used for nuisance parameter estimation, controlling for a high-dimensional set of time-varying confounders (e.g., mean arterial pressure, cerebral oxygen saturation, lactate levels) and baseline covariates to isolate duration's adjusted effect on injury biomarkers (e.g., serum NfL, S100B).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantified, adjusted estimate of hypoxic duration's specific causal contribution to brain injury, moving beyond association. It will clarify whether the relationship is linear or threshold-based, offering a refined model for injury prediction. The application of advanced causal methods in this physiological context will serve as a methodological template for disentangling complex, concurrent insults in critical care research.
proposals.novelty: This study applies state-of-the-art causal inference techniques (TMLE) to a high-stakes clinical physiology problem where traditional regression is insufficient to control for severe time-dependent confounding. It directly addresses the 'adversarial' co-occurrence of insults by framing duration as a treatable exposure, a novel conceptual shift from purely correlative analyses prevalent before 2026.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Temporal Relationship: A Non-Linear and Threshold-Based Analysis of Heart Failure Duration and Cortical Atrophy
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is a recognized risk factor for cognitive decline and brain structural damage, but the precise temporal dynamics remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 clinical studies often treat HF duration as a linear variable, potentially missing critical non-linear or threshold-based relationships that could inform optimal timing for neuroprotective interventions. Clarifying this relationship is crucial for developing targeted strategies to mitigate brain injury in this high-risk population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between HF duration and the magnitude of cortical damage is not linear but follows a threshold or accelerated pattern, where damage progression increases significantly after a specific disease duration or at certain inflection points.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of HF patients with serial brain MRI. Cortical damage will be quantified using automated segmentation (FreeSurfer) to measure cortical thickness and volume. The relationship between HF duration (from diagnosis) and MRI-derived metrics will be analyzed using generalized additive models (GAMs) to identify non-linear patterns and piecewise linear regression to detect potential breakpoints or thresholds. Analyses will adjust for key confounders (age, hypertension, treatment) using multivariate models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a refined, data-driven model of how brain injury accrues over the HF disease course, moving beyond simple linear assumptions. It will identify potential critical time windows for neurological risk, thereby informing the design of future trials for cognitive preservation in HF. The methodological approach integrates clinical phenotyping with advanced neuroimaging and non-linear statistical modeling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes an association between HF and brain damage, this study uniquely applies non-linear and threshold-detection statistical frameworks specifically to disease duration, a modifiable clinical variable, to uncover previously unrecognized temporal dynamics of neural injury.

proposals.title: Ensemble Modeling of Cortical Morphology Trajectories in Heart Failure: A Climate Prediction-Inspired Framework for Uncertainty-Quantified Neurodegeneration Risk
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is increasingly linked to cerebral hypoperfusion and accelerated cortical atrophy, contributing to cognitive decline. However, existing neuroimaging studies often provide cross-sectional snapshots or simple linear models, failing to capture the complex, heterogeneous progression of brain changes and quantify predictive uncertainty. This limits clinical utility for personalized risk stratification and intervention timing.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying an ensemble modeling framework, analogous to multi-model ensembles in climate projection, to longitudinal MRI data will robustly identify consistent, HF-driven trajectories of cortical thinning and reduced gyrification, while quantifying patient-specific uncertainty to distinguish core neurodegenerative pathways from variable, modifiable factors.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal 3T MRI (cortical thickness, local gyrification index) from an existing HF cohort (e.g., from pre-2026 studies like the CAMERA trial). An ensemble of diverse predictive models—including mixed-effects models, Gaussian Process regression, and recurrent neural networks (LSTMs)—will be trained on baseline clinical, biochemical (e.g., NT-proBNP, inflammatory markers), and morphological data to forecast individual trajectories. Climate science-inspired ensemble averaging and variance decomposition techniques will separate robust consensus predictions from inter-model disagreement, identifying stable morphological 'signals' versus uncertain 'noise'.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first uncertainty-quantified, multi-trajectory model of HF-related cortical decline, directly informing clinical prognosis. It will establish a novel computational neuroimaging methodology that translates ensemble forecasting from geosciences to biomedicine, enabling patient stratification by risk of rapid morphological decline.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by explicitly adapting the conceptual and technical framework of climate ensemble prediction—designed for complex, noisy systems—to model brain morphology, moving beyond single-model fits. It integrates Jun Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis of cardiovascular topics and biochemical marker studies (e.g., inflammatory factors) with advanced computational modeling.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Cortical Thinning and Gyrification Decline in Heart Failure: A 3-Year MRI Study Correlated with Serum Inflammatory and Neurofilament Light Chain Levels
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes heart failure (HF) as a risk factor for cognitive decline and brain atrophy, but the spatiotemporal progression of cortical neurodegeneration remains poorly characterized. Understanding the specific morphological trajectories (thickness, gyrification) is critical for identifying imaging biomarkers of HF-related brain injury and linking them to systemic pathophysiology, such as inflammation and axonal damage, which are themes in the researcher's prior work on biochemical levels and treatment effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that HF patients will exhibit accelerated, regionally specific cortical thinning and reduced gyrification over time compared to matched controls, and that the rate of these morphological changes will correlate significantly with longitudinal increases in serum levels of inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) and neurofilament light chain (NfL).
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-year longitudinal cohort study with 120 HF patients and 60 healthy controls, acquiring high-resolution T1-weighted MRI at baseline, 18 months, and 36 months. Cortical reconstruction and thickness/gyrification index calculation will be performed using FreeSurfer (v7.2). Statistical analysis of longitudinal change will employ linear mixed-effects models, with cortical metrics as outcomes and time, group, and their interaction as predictors. Correlation and mediation analyses will assess relationships between morphological change rates and serially measured serum inflammatory markers (via multiplex immunoassay) and NfL (via Simoa), controlling for cardiac function (ejection fraction) and vascular risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed map of progressive cortical morphological changes in HF, defining a timeline for neurodegeneration. It will establish direct links between systemic biochemical dysregulation (inflammation, axonal injury) and in vivo brain structural decline, offering novel, mechanism-informed imaging biomarkers. This bridges cardiovascular neurology and neuroimaging, informing future trials targeting neuroprotection in HF.
proposals.novelty: While cross-sectional brain changes in HF are known, this is a dedicated longitudinal MRI study integrating serial cortical gyrification analysis with targeted serum biomarkers of inflammation and neuronal injury, applying the researcher's expertise in tracking biochemical levels to a neuroimaging context.

proposals.title: Triangulating Causal Inference: Cortical Thinning as an Independent and Etiological Contributor to Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events
proposals.motivation: Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (MACE) risk prediction relies heavily on clinical scores and subclinical vascular markers, yet residual risk remains significant. Pre-2026 neuroimaging studies suggest associations between brain health, particularly cortical atrophy, and cardiovascular outcomes, but the nature of this link—whether it is a mere correlate, a consequence of shared risk factors, or an independent causal pathway—is unclear. Establishing a causal, independent role for brain morphology could unveil a novel, central nervous system-based axis for risk stratification and intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific patterns of cortical morphometry, quantified via MRI, provide information that is both causally relevant to and independent of traditional cardiovascular risk factors and subclinical atherosclerosis for predicting MACE.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data from cohorts with brain MRI and long-term follow-up (e.g., UK Biobank, Rotterdam Study). Cortical thickness/surface area will be extracted using FreeSurfer. The analysis employs a triangulation framework: 1) Cox models with time-lagged imaging to establish temporal sequence, 2) Two-sample Mendelian Randomization using pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics for cortical traits and MACE to assess causality, and 3) Nested model comparison (C-statistic, NRI, IDI) to quantify added predictive value beyond FRS and coronary artery calcium scores. The final model will be developed in a primary cohort and validated for specificity in an independent cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the most rigorous pre-2026 evidence to date on whether the brain is a passive bystander or an active contributor in the cardiovascular disease pathway. It will deliver a validated, multi-modal risk prediction model that quantifies the incremental value of a brain MRI marker. The findings could redefine the neuro-cardiac axis and inform future trials targeting brain health for cardiovascular risk reduction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in discovering an association, but in rigorously dissecting it using a pre-specified multi-method causal triangulation framework (longitudinal, genetic, and predictive) specifically designed to distinguish correlation from independent causal contribution within a comprehensive cardiovascular risk context.

proposals.title: Integrating Cortical Morphology as a Neural Stratigraphy Biomarker for Enhanced Major Adverse Cardiovascular Event (MACE) Risk Prediction: A Multimodal Imaging Study
proposals.motivation: Despite established risk factors, current models for predicting Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (MACE) have limited precision, failing to fully account for the cumulative, systemic impact of vascular disease. The brain, as a highly vascularized end-organ, may harbor structural signatures of long-term vascular insult that are not captured by conventional clinical or serum biomarkers. This gap necessitates the discovery of novel, integrative biomarkers to improve risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: Detailed cortical morphology indices (e.g., thickness, gyrification, surface area), analogous to geotechnical stratigraphy revealing hidden weaknesses, encode the cumulative burden of vascular risk and will significantly improve the predictive accuracy for MACE when integrated with conventional risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using pre-existing datasets (e.g., UK Biobank) containing structural brain MRI and longitudinal health records. Cortical morphology will be quantified using established pipelines like FreeSurfer to extract region-specific metrics. A multimodal prediction model will be built using a machine learning framework (e.g., XGBoost or a Cox regression with LASSO penalty) that integrates these neural indices with conventional factors (e.g., Framingham risk score components, PFAS levels, inflammatory markers). Model performance will be evaluated using time-dependent AUC and net reclassification improvement.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish cortical morphology as a novel, non-invasive biomarker layer for cardiovascular risk, moving beyond current serum-based and vascular imaging paradigms. It will deliver a validated, multimodal risk prediction tool with superior discriminative power, potentially enabling earlier, more targeted preventive interventions.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual analogy to geotechnical stratigraphy analysis and its direct integration of brain structural architecture into cardiovascular risk models, a link not explored in prior computational fluid dynamics or bibliometric studies of atherosclerosis.

proposals.title: Cortical Morphology as a Neurobiological Mediator of Population- and Time-Specific Relationships Between Body Mass Index and Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events
proposals.motivation: The relationship between BMI and cardiovascular risk is inconsistent across populations and historical periods, suggesting a 'one-size-fits-all' BMI cutoff is inadequate. This variability may be driven by differential life-course exposures, including chronic inflammation and stress, which are known to affect both brain structure and cardiovascular health. Understanding the neurobiological substrate linking these exposures to clinical outcomes is a critical pre-2026 gap for personalized risk prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cortical morphology indices (e.g., thickness, surface area, gyrification) mediate the association between BMI and MACE, and that this mediating effect varies significantly across demographic groups (e.g., sex, ethnicity) and birth cohorts, explaining observed heterogeneity in optimal BMI thresholds.
proposals.method: We will integrate large-scale, longitudinal cohort data (e.g., UK Biobank) with structural MRI-derived cortical features. Using causal mediation analysis within a counterfactual framework, we will quantify the proportion of the BMI-MACE relationship mediated by cortical morphology. We will then employ stratified and time-interaction models to test for effect modification by population and birth cohort. Finally, we will develop a prediction model using gradient boosting machines (e.g., XGBoost) that incorporates cortical features to dynamically calibrate BMI-associated risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence for a neurobiological pathway explaining population-specific BMI-MACE relationships, moving risk assessment beyond static BMI cutoffs. It will yield a novel, pathophysiology-aware risk prediction framework that integrates brain structure as a dynamic calibrator of metabolic-cardiovascular risk.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links BMI to brain structure and brain health to cardiovascular outcomes, the explicit test of cortical morphology as a *mediating* and *effect-modifying* variable in the BMI-MACE relationship across populations and time is novel. It uniquely combines neuroimaging epidemiology with causal inference to address a core clinical paradox.

proposals.title: Cortical Morphology as a Novel Imaging Biomarker for Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events: A Machine Learning Prediction Study
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in risk stratification, predicting Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (MACE) remains imperfect. Traditional models rely heavily on clinical and serum biomarkers, such as inflammatory factor levels and bone turnover markers, but lack integration of structural brain data. Given the established brain-heart axis and the role of cerebral small vessel disease in cardiovascular outcomes, cortical morphology may offer a novel, non-invasive predictive dimension that is currently underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that indices of cortical morphology (e.g., cortical thickness, surface area, and gyrification), derived from structural MRI, contain independent prognostic information and will significantly improve the predictive accuracy for MACE beyond established clinical and biochemical risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using existing datasets (e.g., UK Biobank) with linked brain MRI and longitudinal health records. Cortical reconstruction and parcellation will be performed using FreeSurfer (v7.2) to extract morphology indices. We will employ a machine learning pipeline, comparing a baseline Cox proportional hazards model with traditional risk factors (e.g., age, lipids, serum inflammatory markers) against an enhanced model incorporating cortical features. Feature selection will use LASSO regression, and model performance will be evaluated using time-dependent AUC and net reclassification improvement.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive assessment of cortical morphology as a predictor of hard cardiovascular endpoints. It will deliver a validated, multi-modal risk prediction model and potentially identify specific neuroanatomical signatures of heightened cardiovascular risk, bridging neuroimaging and cardiology research.
proposals.novelty: While computational fluid dynamics and bibliometric analyses have advanced atherosclerosis research, the direct use of brain structural metrics for MACE prediction is novel. This approach uniquely combines Jun Ma's expertise in biomarker level analysis and advanced computational methods to interrogate the brain-heart connection.

proposals.title: Causal Effects of Guideline-Directed Heart Failure Therapy on Cortical Thinning: A Target Trial Emulation and Instrumental Variable Analysis
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is strongly associated with cognitive decline and accelerated brain atrophy, but it is unclear whether this is a direct consequence of the disease or influenced by its treatment. Guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) improves cardiac output and may affect cerebral perfusion, yet its causal impact on brain structure remains unknown. Establishing a causal link is critical for understanding the brain-heart axis and could inform therapeutic strategies to protect neurocognitive health in HF patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initiation and intensification of GDMT for heart failure causally attenuates the rate of cortical thinning compared to less intensive treatment regimens, independent of measured and unmeasured confounding factors.
proposals.method: We will emulate a target trial using longitudinal electronic health records linked to neuroimaging data (e.g., from UK Biobank or similar cohorts). New users of different GDMT combinations (e.g., ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blockers, MRAs, SGLT2 inhibitors) will be matched using time-varying propensity scores based on clinical and demographic confounders. The primary outcome is the trajectory of cortical thickness measured via serial MRI. To address residual confounding, we will implement an instrumental variable (IV) analysis, leveraging regional variation in physician prescribing preference as the instrument, and estimate causal effects using two-stage least squares regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal estimates of HF treatment on brain structure, moving beyond observational associations. It will demonstrate the application of advanced causal inference methods (target trial emulation and IV analysis) to the neuro-cardiac domain. The findings could identify a novel neuroprotective benefit of specific HF therapies, informing future clinical guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked HF to brain changes, this study uniquely applies a formal causal inference framework to isolate the treatment effect. The combination of target trial emulation for measured confounders with instrumental variable analysis to address unmeasured confounding in a neuroimaging context is a novel methodological integration.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Forecasting Model to Project Population-Level Cortical Thinning from Heart Failure Treatment Policy Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence establishes a strong bidirectional link between heart failure (HF) and cognitive decline, with HF-associated hypoperfusion accelerating cortical thinning. However, healthcare policy decisions for HF are rarely evaluated for their long-term impact on brain health outcomes. This creates a missed opportunity to quantify the neuroprotective return on investment from scaling up evidence-based cardiovascular care, a critical gap in integrated public health strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating longitudinal neuroimaging and clinical registry data within a Bayesian forecasting framework will generate statistically robust, probabilistic projections showing that increased penetration of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for HF significantly reduces the future combined population prevalence of HF and associated cortical thinning over a 10-year horizon.
proposals.method: We will construct a Bayesian hierarchical model using longitudinal data from cohorts like the Framingham Heart Study, integrating serial cardiac MRI/echocardiography, brain MRI-derived cortical thickness measures, and treatment records. The model will employ Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling (via Stan) to estimate posterior distributions linking GDMT adherence levels to rates of cortical thinning. These estimated parameters will drive a forecasting module that simulates population-level outcomes under different policy-driven GDMT scaling scenarios, generating probabilistic forecasts with credible intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative tool for policymakers to forecast the brain health gains of HF treatment investments, moving beyond cardiovascular outcomes alone. It will contribute a novel, clinically grounded modeling framework that directly connects specific treatment levers (e.g., SGLT2 inhibitor uptake) to probabilistic trajectories of brain morphology, supporting more holistic economic evaluations of healthcare interventions.
proposals.novelty: While computational models exist for HF progression and separate models for brain atrophy, the fusion of these into a policy-responsive Bayesian forecasting system for population-level brain health is novel. This approach uniquely quantifies the downstream neuroprotective effects of upstream cardiovascular policy decisions, a synthesis not yet attempted in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Pre-registered, Multi-Cohort Stratified Discovery and Validation of PFAS-Associated Inflammatory and Bone Turnover Biomarkers in Heart Failure Subtypes
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is a heterogeneous syndrome, and the role of environmental exposures like per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in specific HF subtypes remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 evidence links PFAS exposure to dysregulated inflammation and bone metabolism, processes implicated in cardiovascular health, but studies lack rigorous, subtype-stratified designs. This gap limits the identification of actionable, etiology-specific biomarkers for risk stratification and targeted intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that serum PFAS levels are associated with distinct profiles of inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) and bone turnover markers (e.g., P1NP, B-CTx) in a HF subtype-specific manner, and that these associations can be robustly validated in independent cohorts.
proposals.method: We will employ a pre-registered, two-phase design. In the discovery phase, using a deeply phenotyped cohort (e.g., from the Framingham Heart Study), we will perform stratified linear regression analyses across pre-defined HF etiologies (e.g., ischemic vs. non-ischemic) and NYHA severity bins, testing associations between PFAS levels and a panel of serum inflammatory factors and bone markers. Subtype-specific hypotheses and statistical thresholds (corrected via Benjamini-Hochberg FDR) will be pre-specified. These will be tested in one or more independent validation cohorts (e.g., ARIC, MESA) using identical, locked-down models to ensure reproducibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, subtype-specific map of PFAS-associated biochemical perturbations in HF, moving beyond generic associations. It will provide a rigorous methodological template for exposure biomarker research in heterogeneous diseases and identify potential novel, mechanistically informed therapeutic targets (e.g., bone-modifying or anti-inflammatory agents) for specific HF patient subgroups.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a pre-registered, stratified, multi-cohort validation framework—common in genomics—into the study of environmental chemical exposures in HF, directly addressing concerns about data dredging and reproducibility. It uniquely bridges Dr. Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis, inflammatory/bone markers (e.g., BGP, P1NP), and PFAS research within a robust causal inference design.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Multi-Center Validation of Inflammatory and Metabolic Biomarkers in Heart Failure Using an Adaptive, Etiology-Stratified Cohort Design
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is a heterogeneous syndrome with diverse etiologies, yet most biomarker studies treat it as a monolithic entity, leading to inconsistent findings and failed therapeutic trials. Pre-2026, oncology has demonstrated the critical importance of biomarker validation within precisely defined patient subgroups for successful drug development. Applying this stratified, precision medicine framework to HF is essential to identify which biomarkers are generalizable and which are specific to underlying pathophysiology, such as ischemic injury, genetic cardiomyopathy, or infiltrative diseases like cardiac amyloidosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that previously reported associations between systemic inflammatory markers (e.g., specific cytokines) and metabolic markers (e.g., bone turnover markers like BGP or P1NP, by analogy to vascular calcification) with HF outcomes are not uniformly generalizable but are significantly driven by specific, adjudicated etiologic and severity-based subgroups.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective, multi-center cohort of 1200 HF patients with reduced ejection fraction. Enrollment will use an adaptive enrichment framework with pre-specified targets for key etiologies: ischemic, genetic dilated cardiomyopathy (with genetic testing), and cardiac amyloidosis (confirmed by scintigraphy/biopsy). All patients undergo centralized core lab analysis of echocardiography and cardiac MRI for consistent phenotyping. Serum will be analyzed in batch for a panel of inflammatory factors (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) and bone/vascular metabolic markers (BGP, P1NP, B-CTx). Statistical analysis will employ interaction tests and machine learning (e.g., random forest with subgroup stratification) to identify biomarker-by-subgroup interactions for the primary composite endpoint of HF hospitalization or cardiovascular death.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a robust, etiology-stratified validation of candidate HF biomarkers, moving beyond simple association to mechanistic context. It will directly inform the design of future targeted clinical trials by identifying which patient subgroups are most likely to benefit from specific anti-inflammatory or metabolic interventions. The established adaptive, core lab-based cohort framework will serve as a model for future precision studies in cardiovascular disease.
proposals.novelty: This work novelly adapts the oncology trial paradigm of centralized pathology and biomarker-driven patient stratification to heart failure epidemiology, creating a validation cohort designed a priori to test for etiologic heterogeneity rather than discover it post-hoc.

proposals.title: Stratified Analysis of Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substance (PFAS) Levels and Inflammatory Biomarkers in a Large Heart Failure Cohort: Etiology and Severity as Key Determinants
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is a heterogeneous syndrome with distinct etiologies (e.g., ischemic, non-ischemic) and severity levels, which may influence systemic inflammation and response to environmental exposures. Pre-2026 research has linked PFAS exposure to adverse cardiovascular outcomes and altered inflammatory pathways, but studies in HF populations are limited and often treat HF as a monolithic entity. A detailed, stratified analysis is crucial to identify specific patient subgroups most vulnerable to the cardiometabolic effects of PFAS, informing targeted monitoring and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that circulating levels of specific PFAS compounds will correlate significantly with serum inflammatory factor levels (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) and clinical outcomes in HF patients, and that the strength and nature of these associations will differ meaningfully based on HF etiology (ischemic vs. non-ischemic) and severity (NYHA class, ejection fraction).
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, cross-sectional and longitudinal study enrolling a minimum of 800 HF patients. Participants will be stratified by primary etiology (ischemic, hypertensive, idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy) and severity (NYHA class, LVEF). Serum will be analyzed for a panel of legacy and emerging PFAS (e.g., PFOA, PFOS, GenX) using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Concurrently, a multiplex immunoassay will quantify serum inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, CRP) and bone/cardiovascular markers (BGP, P1NP). Statistical analysis will employ multivariate linear and logistic regression models, adjusting for confounders, to test for interactions between PFAS levels, HF strata, and biomarker/outcome variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, etiology-stratified profile of PFAS body burden in a HF population, directly linking specific compounds to inflammatory dysregulation within defined pathophysiological contexts. It will generate hypothesis-driving data on whether certain HF subtypes exhibit heightened biological susceptibility to PFAS, potentially revealing novel environmental determinants of disease progression. The findings will advance the field of environmental cardiology from broad associations towards precision public health insights.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links PFAS to general cardiovascular risk, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed HF phenotyping with advanced environmental chemical analysis to dissect heterogeneity in exposure-response relationships, a non-obvious layer of complexity overlooked in prior epidemiological studies.

proposals.title: Association between cortical thinning and subclinical cardiac dysfunction: A multimodal imaging and serum biomarker study
proposals.motivation: The brain-heart axis is a critical frontier in systemic medicine, with established links between stroke, dementia, and cardiovascular health.
