However, the potential for primary neurodegenerative cortical thinning, a hallmark of conditions like early Alzheimer's, to be associated with subclinical cardiac dysfunction remains underexplored. This is significant as it could reveal a shared pathophysiology or a novel cardiac risk factor in neurological patients, impacting their holistic management.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with primary cortical thinning, as quantified by structural MRI, will exhibit significantly impaired left ventricular global longitudinal strain (GLS) on echocardiography and elevated serum levels of inflammatory and cardiac stress biomarkers (e.g., NT-proBNP, hs-CRP) compared to age-matched controls, indicating subclinical cardiac dysfunction.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 patients with primary cortical thinning (confirmed via T1-weighted MRI and FreeSurfer analysis for cortical thickness) and 30 healthy controls. All participants will undergo comprehensive transthoracic echocardiography, with a primary focus on 2D speckle-tracking echocardiography to measure left ventricular GLS, a sensitive marker of subclinical dysfunction. Serum will be analyzed for NT-proBNP, hs-CRP, and other relevant markers (e.g., IL-6). Statistical analysis will employ linear regression models to test the association between regional cortical thickness and cardiac parameters, controlling for traditional cardiovascular risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative evidence linking the severity of cortical neurodegeneration to subclinical cardiac mechanical impairment. It will generate a novel biomarker profile for brain-heart axis dysfunction and could establish a rationale for early cardiac screening in patients with cortical thinning, potentially altering clinical management paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While prior research links overt cardiac events to brain health, this study uniquely investigates the reverse: whether a primary brain structural deficit predicts subtle cardiac dysfunction. It combines advanced neuroimaging (FreeSurfer) with state-of-the-art cardiac mechanics (speckle-tracking) in a targeted population, moving beyond epidemiological associations to mechanistic, multimodal correlation.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Validation of Mendelian Randomization: A Prospective Cohort Study of Bone Mineral Density Trajectories in Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 Mendelian Randomization (MR) studies have suggested a causal link between type 2 diabetes (T2D) and altered bone mineral density (BMD), but these findings require validation in real-world clinical populations. Observational data on BMD changes in diabetic patients are often conflicting, and the influence of specific treatments, like SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists, on bone turnover remains incompletely characterized. Prospective validation is essential to translate genetic epidemiology into actionable clinical insights for fracture risk management in this growing patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal BMD loss in patients with T2D will be significantly greater than in non-diabetic controls, and that this trajectory will be associated with specific patterns of serum bone turnover markers (e.g., P1NP, β-CTx) and inflammatory factors (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α), thereby corroborating prior MR-derived causal pathways.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective, multi-center observational cohort of 800 postmenopausal women and men over 50, stratified by T2D status and treatment regimen. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) scans and serum collection (for BGP, P1NP, β-CTx, and inflammatory cytokines) will be performed at baseline and at 24-month intervals for 4 years. We will employ linear mixed-effects models to analyze BMD trajectory differences, adjusting for covariates (age, BMI, renal function, medication use). Mediation analysis will test if changes in specific serum markers (identified in prior MR studies) statistically explain the association between T2D status and BMD decline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal clinical evidence directly validating MR-predicted causal effects of T2D on bone health. It will delineate the real-world pace of BMD loss in T2D and identify modifiable biochemical mediators (inflammatory and bone turnover factors) linking diabetes to skeletal fragility. The findings will inform targeted monitoring strategies and the design of future intervention trials to mitigate fracture risk in diabetic patients.
proposals.novelty: While MR studies imply causality, this work uniquely bridges genetic epidemiology and clinical diabetology by prospectively tracking the precise biochemical and densitometric sequelae of T2D, integrating specific drug exposures as effect modifiers—a layer of complexity not captured in standard MR analyses.

proposals.title: Non-linear Mendelian Randomization Reveals the Dose-Response Relationship Between Type 2 Diabetes Liability and Bone Mineral Density
proposals.motivation: The epidemiological association between type 2 diabetes (T2D) and increased bone mineral density (BMD) is well-documented, yet the causal nature and shape of this relationship remain unclear. Traditional linear Mendelian randomization (MR) assumes a constant effect, potentially obscuring clinically relevant non-linear or threshold effects that could inform targeted interventions for bone health in diabetic patients. Clarifying this dose-response curve is critical for understanding the paradoxical fracture risk in T2D and for developing precision-based skeletal management strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the causal effect of genetically proxied T2D liability on BMD is non-linear, exhibiting a threshold or plateau effect where the BMD benefit diminishes or reverses at higher levels of diabetic risk.
proposals.method: We will apply established non-linear MR methods, specifically the residual and doubly-ranked stratification approaches, using large-scale genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics for T2D and BMD at key skeletal sites (e.g., femoral neck, lumbar spine). Genetic variants will be used as instrumental variables for T2D liability. We will model the shape of the exposure-outcome curve using fractional polynomial models and test for non-linearity against a linear null hypothesis. Sensitivity analyses will include MR-Egger and weighted median methods to assess robustness to pleiotropy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first genetically informed, non-linear causal estimate of the T2D-BMD relationship, moving beyond a simple binary or linear association. It will clarify whether skeletal benefits are proportional to diabetic risk or concentrated in specific risk strata, offering mechanistic insights and a foundation for risk-stratified clinical guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While Jun Ma's prior work analyzed biochemical markers like BGP and CTX in treatment contexts, this proposal innovatively applies cutting-edge, pre-2026 non-linear MR methodology—a significant advance over standard MR—to a clinically paradoxical relationship, integrating genetic epidemiology with bone endocrinology.

proposals.title: Mapping the Dual Burden of Malnutrition: A Multi-Parameter Phenotype Chart for Public Health Screening
proposals.motivation: The global rise of the dual burden of malnutrition, where undernutrition and obesity co-exist, challenges traditional BMI-based classification, which fails to identify high-risk phenotypes like 'normal-weight obesity' or 'metabolically obese, normal weight'. This limitation hinders precise public health screening and targeted interventions. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need for more nuanced anthropometric tools that integrate multiple body composition measures to better capture metabolic risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-parameter composite index, visualized as a 2D/3D phenotype chart using axes such as BMI, waist circumference, and body fat percentage, will more accurately categorize individuals into distinct malnutrition risk phenotypes than BMI alone, and these phenotypes will correlate significantly with adverse biochemical profiles (e.g., inflammatory markers, lipid levels).
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional analysis of existing population health datasets (e.g., NHANES) containing BMI, waist circumference, bioelectrical impedance-based body fat percentage, and key serum biomarkers (inflammatory factors, lipid profiles). Using principal component analysis (PCA) to reduce dimensionality, we will develop a composite score. Individuals will be plotted on a 2D chart (BMI vs. waist-to-height ratio) and a 3D scatter plot (adding body fat %), with regions defined via k-means clustering. The correlation between cluster membership and biochemical profiles will be tested using multivariate regression, adjusting for confounders like age and sex.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, practical visualization tool for public health that moves beyond single-metric classification, enabling more precise identification of at-risk groups for the dual burden of malnutrition. It will establish empirical evidence linking specific multi-parameter phenotypes to adverse metabolic health indicators, informing tailored screening protocols.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual translation of materials science composite indices and Ashby-style performance charts to human health phenotyping, creating a multi-axial mapping solution for a complex public health problem not addressed by existing anthropometric indices.

proposals.title: Tracing the Lexical Shift: NLP Analysis of Policy Discourse on Body Composition and its Correlation with Dual Burden of Malnutrition Metrics
proposals.motivation: The dual burden of malnutrition, encompassing undernutrition and overnutrition, is a critical global health challenge. Pre-2026, clinical and public health discourse increasingly questions the adequacy of BMI alone, advocating for metrics like waist circumference and body fat percentage to better assess metabolic health risks. However, the evolution of this discourse within authoritative policy documents and its potential influence on population-level health measurement and outcomes remains unquantified.
proposals.hypothesis: The linguistic evolution in national health policies—specifically, a relative increase in mentions of alternative body composition metrics (e.g., waist-to-hip ratio) compared to BMI—precedes or coincides with measurable shifts in the prevalence of the dual burden of malnutrition as defined by these more nuanced metrics in national health surveys.
proposals.method: We will apply Natural Language Processing (NLP) to a longitudinal corpus of national health strategies and clinical guidelines. Using pre-trained transformer models like BERT for contextual embedding, we will perform targeted entity recognition and sentiment/context analysis around body composition terms. Time-series regression models (e.g., ARIMA) will correlate the derived linguistic trends (e.g., term frequency-inverse document frequency evolution) with corresponding national survey data on malnutrition prevalence, controlling for confounders like GDP and healthcare expenditure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence linking the semantic content of health policy to conceptual shifts in measuring a major epidemiological transition. It will create a novel methodological framework for analyzing policy impact via text, offering public health officials a data-driven tool to evaluate and craft more precise health communication.
proposals.novelty: While bibliometric analysis of research trends (as in the researcher's prior work) is common, applying NLP to trace the evolution of specific clinical concepts within policy language and directly linking it to population health metrics is a novel, interdisciplinary fusion of computational linguistics and public health epidemiology.

proposals.title: Mapping the Central Autonomic Connectome to Derive Person-Specific Cardiometabolic Risk Thresholds: A Network Neuroscience Approach in Heart Failure
proposals.motivation: Conventional cardiometabolic risk stratification relies on static population-level metrics like BMI, which fail to account for significant individual variability in neurovisceral integration. Pre-2026 research highlights the brain-heart axis's role in metabolic regulation and the clinical heterogeneity of conditions like heart failure, yet a framework for translating individual brain network architecture into personalized health benchmarks is absent. This gap limits the precision of preventive and therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional and structural architecture of an individual's central autonomic network (the brain-heart connectome) defines a neural phenotype that determines their personalized optimal range for body composition and metabolic markers, rendering conventional static BMI cutoffs suboptimal for risk prediction in this subgroup.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of heart failure patients and at-risk individuals, collecting multimodal MRI (resting-state fMRI for functional connectivity, DTI for structural connectivity) and detailed cardiometabolic profiles (BMI, inflammatory markers like serum IFcs, BGP, B-CTx, P1NP). Using pre-2026 network neuroscience methods, we will construct individual brain-heart connectomes, applying graph theory analysis and dynamic causal modeling to quantify network integrity. Machine learning algorithms (e.g., support vector regression, random forests) will model the relationship between connectome features and individual deviations from population-level BMI-risk associations to identify neural circuit signatures of personalized risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, neurobiologically-grounded biomarker framework for precision cardiometabolic medicine, moving beyond epidemiology to individual pathophysiology. It will deliver specific brain network signatures that can stratify patients for whom traditional BMI thresholds are misleading, directly informing targeted interventions. The methodology integrates Dr. Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis, biochemical markers, and computational modeling for a translational outcome.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of dynamic BMI threshold concepts with quantitative brain-heart connectomics to generate person-specific health benchmarks is a novel paradigm. While connectomics and cardiometabolic risk are studied separately, their integration to redefine a clinical index (BMI) based on central nervous system architecture is a non-obvious, systems-level approach to precision medicine.

proposals.title: Modeling Population Dynamics of Body Mass Index Categories: A Markov Chain and Computational Fluid Dynamics-Inspired Analysis of Transition Flows
proposals.motivation: Current public health metrics for obesity primarily rely on static prevalence rates, which mask the underlying dynamics of weight change within populations. This static view limits the ability to forecast future disease burdens and to evaluate the specific impact of interventions on promoting healthy weight loss or preventing unhealthy weight gain. A dynamic, flow-based model is needed to understand the 'traffic' between BMI categories, analogous to understanding cellular or molecular fluxes in disease progression.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of Markov chain models, informed by principles from computational fluid dynamics used in atherosclerosis research, can accurately quantify the rates of individual transitions between BMI categories over time. We hypothesize that these transition rates will be significantly associated with specific biochemical markers of metabolic health and inflammation.
proposals.method: We will utilize longitudinal cohort data (e.g., NHANES, UK Biobank) to construct discrete-time Markov chain models, where states are defined BMI categories (underweight, normal, overweight, obese). Transition probability matrices will be estimated for different demographic strata. Inspired by CFD applications in atherosclerosis, we will model the 'flow' of individuals between states, analyzing stability and pressure points. Associations between transition probabilities and serum levels of inflammatory factors (e.g., CRP, IL-6), PFAS, and bone turnover markers (like P1NP, B-CTx) will be tested using multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic framework for obesity epidemiology, moving beyond prevalence to reveal the net flows driving population-level changes. It will identify which demographic and biochemical factors are most strongly associated with transitions into higher-risk BMI categories, offering targeted intervention points. The methodological fusion of population health modeling with CFD-inspired flux analysis represents a significant interdisciplinary advance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the analytical focus from static category membership to dynamic transition flows, and in the conceptual translation of computational fluid dynamics principles—used to study particle flux in arteries—to model human movement between health states. This approach is non-obvious in standard epidemiological practice, which treats BMI categories as fixed compartments.

proposals.title: Integrating Early Childhood into Global BMI Surveillance: A Novel Application of WHO Growth Standard Z-Scores for Trend Analysis
proposals.motivation: The global double burden of malnutrition begins in early childhood, yet children under five are systematically excluded from standard global BMI trend analyses due to their non-linear growth. This creates a critical data gap in understanding the developmental origins of obesity and undernutrition. Pre-2026, large-scale surveys like DHS and MICS collect anthropometric data for this age group, but lack a standardized, longitudinal metric for population-level trend analysis comparable to adult BMI.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the WHO Child Growth Standards to calculate BMI-for-age Z-scores for children under five in existing global health surveys will yield a valid, normalized metric. This will enable the detection of significant temporal and cross-national trends in underweight and overweight prevalence in this population, previously obscured by non-comparable raw measures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of publicly available pre-2026 survey data (e.g., DHS, MICS). For each child under five with recorded age, sex, weight, and height, we will compute BMI and then calculate the BMI-for-age Z-score (BAZ) using the WHO 2006 Growth Standards as the reference population, implemented via the WHO Anthro software or its algorithmic equations. We will aggregate individual BAZ values to calculate population prevalence of underweight (BAZ < -2 SD) and overweight (BAZ > +2 SD). Time-trend analysis will be performed using joinpoint regression to identify significant changes in prevalence across survey waves.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first methodological framework for seamlessly integrating early childhood into longitudinal, global BMI surveillance systems. It will produce novel, comparable estimates of underweight and overweight trends for children under five across diverse countries, filling a major gap in public health metrics. The findings will inform earlier-targeted interventions and refine global burden of disease estimates related to malnutrition.
proposals.novelty: While Z-scores are standard for cross-sectional child nutrition assessment, their systematic application for analyzing *longitudinal population trends* in global BMI surveillance is novel. This adapts a pharmaco-statistical method (developmental normalization) to solve a public health analytics problem, creating a developmentally appropriate bridge between pediatric and adult epidemiological metrics.

proposals.title: Network Diffusion of Cortical Thinning in Heart Failure: A Structural Connectome-Based Test of the Secondary Neurodegeneration Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is strongly associated with cognitive decline and brain atrophy, but the causal mechanism linking cardiac dysfunction to cortical thinning remains unclear. Pre-2026 research suggests a vascular/metabolic 'insult' may initiate the process, yet the spatial pattern of atrophy is not fully explained by vascular territories, hinting at a network-dependent progression. This project addresses a critical gap by formally modeling whether atrophy in HF spreads via the brain's structural wiring, moving beyond correlative descriptions to a mechanistic, network-based framework.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial pattern of cortical thinning in heart failure patients is best predicted by a network diffusion process on the structural connectome, seeded in regions of initial functional vulnerability, supporting the model that atrophy is a consequence of network-level spread rather than a direct, simultaneous cause of cognitive symptoms.
proposals.method: We will acquire multi-shell diffusion MRI and T1-weighted structural MRI from a pre-existing cohort of HF patients and matched controls. The structural connectome will be reconstructed using tractography (e.g., MRtrix3). Cortical thickness will be measured from T1 images (FreeSurfer). We will apply a network diffusion model (e.g., a graph Laplacian-based heat kernel) to simulate atrophy spread, using regions showing early metabolic dysfunction (from available literature or derived from resting-state fMRI) as seed points. The model's predicted atrophy pattern will be statistically compared to the observed pattern using spatial correlation and node-level regression, controlling for vascular risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, model-based test of the network-spread hypothesis for brain atrophy in HF, potentially identifying a key mechanism linking cardiac and brain health. It will generate a predictive framework for atrophy progression, which could inform future neuroprotective trials. The synthesis of network science with clinical neuroimaging directly addresses the causal question of neurodegeneration in systemic disease.
proposals.novelty: While network diffusion models have been applied to neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's, their application to heart failure-related brain atrophy is novel. The approach uniquely integrates the concept of a systemic, non-structural 'insult' with subsequent connectome-guided progression, moving beyond static correlation to model dynamic spread.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Neuro-Policy Analysis: Correlating National Health Policy Language with Cortical Atrophy Trajectories in Heart Failure
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) is a systemic syndrome with well-documented cognitive impairment and brain structural changes, including cortical thinning. Concurrently, national health policies shape clinical practice and resource allocation. However, no study has quantitatively linked the evolving language of these high-level policy frameworks to longitudinal neuroanatomical outcomes in a chronic disease cohort, creating a critical gap in understanding how macro-level healthcare directives might biologically manifest.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that temporal shifts in the semantic content of national HF and cardiovascular disease policies—specifically increased emphasis on integrated mental health care and patient self-management—will correlate with attenuated rates of cortical thinning in prefrontal and limbic regions in a longitudinal HF patient cohort.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-year longitudinal study in 150 HF patients, acquiring annual 3T MRI scans to compute cortical thickness (FreeSurfer v7.2). Concurrently, we will apply NLP (BERT-based topic modeling and sentiment analysis) to a corpus of U.S. national policy documents (CMS guidelines, AHA statements, NIH strategic plans) published from 2015-2025. Dynamic time-lagged regression models will correlate annualized policy topic prevalence (e.g., 'mental health,' 'rehabilitation,' 'guideline adherence') with annualized rates of regional cortical thinning, controlling for clinical covariates (ejection fraction, NYHA class, treatment).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework linking health policy linguistics to neurobiological trajectories, offering a novel 'policy biomarker' for population health neuroscience. It will generate actionable insights for policymakers on the potential neurological impact of policy wording and priorities, directly extending Dr. Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis and biochemical markers to a novel, systems-level intervention analysis.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of computational policy NLP with longitudinal neuroimaging in a clinical cohort is unprecedented. It moves beyond studying direct clinical interventions to investigate how the language of systemic governance might indirectly influence brain health, creating a new methodology at the intersection of health policy analysis and clinical neuroscience.

proposals.title: Mapping the High-Resolution Brain-Heart Connectome in Heart Failure: A Network Neuroscience Approach Linking Specific Autonomic Subnetworks to Clinical Phenotypes
proposals.motivation: Heart failure (HF) involves profound dysregulation of the brain-heart axis, but current neuroimaging studies often treat the brain as a coarse, monolithic network. This limits mechanistic understanding and the development of targeted neuromodulation. A high-resolution, subnetwork-level analysis is needed to pinpoint the specific neural circuits whose dysfunction drives autonomic imbalance and adverse outcomes in HF patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, high-resolution neural subnetworks governing autonomic, baroreceptive, and interoceptive functions will exhibit specific, quantifiable patterns of aberrant connectivity in HF, and that these patterns will correlate with granular clinical phenotypes, including inflammatory marker levels (e.g., serum IFs, BGP) and treatment response variability.
proposals.method: We will acquire high-resolution resting-state and task-based fMRI from HF patients and matched controls. Using the Human Connectome Project's multimodal parcellation (Glasser et al., 2016) and brainstem-specific atlases, we will construct individual whole-brain connectomes. Graph theory metrics (e.g., modularity, participation coefficient) and seed-based connectivity will be applied to predefined autonomic subnetworks (e.g., central autonomic network nodes: insula, anterior cingulate, amygdala, periaqueductal gray, nucleus tractus solitarius). These neural metrics will be correlated with clinical data (e.g., heart rate variability, NT-proBNP, inflammatory factors) using partial least squares correlation, controlling for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution map of brain-heart connectome dysfunction in HF, moving beyond regional activation to circuit-level connectivity. It will identify specific neural subnetworks as biomarkers for HF subtypes and progression. The findings will establish a more precise neurobiological foundation for emerging neuromodulatory treatments, such as vagus nerve stimulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a high-resolution, subnetwork-specific network neuroscience framework—inspired by 'subnational analysis' principles—to the brain-heart axis, integrating it with detailed biochemical profiles (e.g., inflammatory factors, bone turnover markers) from the researcher's prior work to uncover mechanistically informed, circuit-based biomarkers.

proposals.title: Modeling the National Optimal BMI Distribution for Minimized Disease Burden: A Computational and Epidemiological Framework
proposals.motivation: Current public health guidelines define individual-level optimal BMI ranges, but the population-level BMI distribution that minimizes total disease burden remains undefined. This gap is critical for strategic resource allocation and policy planning, especially given the rising global prevalence of obesity and its associated metabolic and cardiovascular diseases. A systematic model is needed to move from individual risk assessment to population health optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the national BMI distribution that minimizes total disease burden is not a single-point target but a right-skewed distribution with a lower mean than the current observed distribution, and that this optimal distribution can be derived by integrating disease-specific risk functions with demographic and comorbidity data.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational modeling framework integrating large-scale epidemiological datasets (e.g., NHANES, UK Biobank). Disease burden will be modeled as a function of BMI for key conditions (e.g., myocardial infarction, type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis) using established risk functions from pre-2026 meta-analyses. We will use constrained optimization algorithms (e.g., gradient descent within a Monte Carlo simulation framework) to iteratively adjust a parameterized national BMI distribution (modeled as a skewed Gaussian) to minimize the aggregate, age-adjusted Disability-Adjusted Life Year (DALY) output. Sensitivity analyses will assess the influence of PFAS exposure levels and inflammatory markers (e.g., serum IFcs) as effect modifiers on the BMI-disease risk curves.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model of a population-level optimal BMI distribution, offering a novel target for public health policy beyond individual guidelines. It will establish a methodological framework for translating individual risk data into population health optimization. The results will directly inform cost-benefit analyses for national health interventions aimed at shifting the population BMI curve.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the classic epidemiological question from 'what is the disease risk at a given BMI?' to 'what BMI distribution minimizes total disease?', applying computational optimization techniques from engineering (evident in the PI's CFD bibliometric work) to a core public health problem. It uniquely integrates comorbidity and biochemical modifier data (e.g., inflammatory factors) into the population-level optimization model.

proposals.title: Household Nutritional Polarization and Cardiometabolic Stress: A Life-Course Investigation of Subclinical Cardiac Dysfunction and Neurostructural Correlates
proposals.motivation: The global rise of the 'dual burden' household, where undernutrition and overnutrition co-exist, represents a novel social determinant of health not captured by individual-level metrics. Pre-2026 research links both underweight and obesity to cardiometabolic risk and systemic inflammation, which are implicated in subclinical cardiac dysfunction and brain atrophy. However, the household as a shared stress environment and its life-course impact on early, pre-symptomatic organ damage remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that household-level nutritional polarization (co-occurring underweight and obesity) creates a shared inflammatory and cardiometabolic stress environment, leading to subclinical left ventricular dysfunction in all adult members. We further hypothesize that this early cardiac dysfunction is associated with accelerated cortical thinning and reduced gray matter volume, indicating a shared pathway of cardiocerebral vulnerability.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional household study, recruiting 300 multi-generational households. Household cardiometabolic stress will be indexed by the co-occurrence of underweight (BMI<18.5) and obesity (BMI≥30) among adult members. Subclinical cardiac function will be assessed via speckle-tracking echocardiography for global longitudinal strain (GLS). Brain structure will be quantified using 3T MRI for cortical thickness and volume (FreeSurfer v7.2). We will employ multilevel mixed-effects models to account for household clustering, adjusting for age, sex, and individual cardiovascular risk factors. Mediation analysis will test if serum inflammatory markers (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α) and metabolic biomarkers (e.g., PFAS levels, given their link to cardiometabolic disruption) mediate the relationship between household status and neurostructural outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence linking household nutritional composition to subclinical multi-organ pathology, shifting the focus from individual to shared environmental risk. It will identify specific imaging and biochemical signatures of early cardiocerebral decline driven by social disparities. The findings will inform targeted, household-based public health interventions aimed at preventing downstream heart failure and cognitive decline.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its conceptualization of the household as a unit of cardiometabolic stress and its integration of advanced cardiac strain imaging with neurostructural metrics to capture pre-clinical disease. It uniquely applies a life-course, multi-system framework to the dual burden phenomenon, moving beyond descriptive epidemiology to mechanistic, organ-level investigation.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Hierarchical Model to Quantify and Correct Protocol-Dependent Bias in Blood Pressure Meta-Analyses
proposals.motivation: Meta-analyses of blood pressure (BP) effects on cardiovascular outcomes, such as atherosclerosis, are confounded by systematic measurement differences across studies (e.g., clinic vs. ambulatory, device type, rest period). Pre-2026, these are often treated as random noise, biasing pooled effect estimates and obscuring true exposure-response relationships. This is analogous to the need for standardized biomarker measurement in PFAS health studies or bone turnover marker analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of between-study heterogeneity in BP-associated risk estimates is attributable to systematic, quantifiable biases from measurement protocols, and that explicitly modeling these biases will improve the accuracy and clinical utility of meta-analytic findings.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical meta-regression model. The core model will treat study-specific BP effect estimates as outcomes, with measurement protocol features (e.g., setting, device, operator) as bias-inducing covariates. We will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling via Stan to estimate protocol-specific bias parameters and posterior distributions for a corrected, consensus exposure-effect relationship. Model validation will use simulation studies and a case-study meta-analysis of systolic BP and carotid intima-media thickness, leveraging data from pre-2026 systematic reviews.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal statistical framework to disentangle true biological signal from measurement artifact in pooled cardiovascular epidemiology. It will yield a tool for sensitivity analysis and bias correction in future meta-analyses, improving the evidence base for treatment guidelines. The approach is generalizable to other clinical domains where measurement heterogeneity is a concern.
proposals.novelty: While meta-regression is common, pre-2026 methods rarely model measurement protocol as a structured, bias-generating process within a Bayesian framework that propagates uncertainty. This integrates concepts from clinical measurement science (as seen in bone marker research) into computational epidemiology, moving beyond the standard assumption of uniform measurement error.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Bias: A Meta-Analytic and Bibliometric Assessment of How Clinical and Non-Community Samples Distort Global Estimates of PFAS Exposure and Inflammatory Biomarker Levels
proposals.motivation: Pooled analyses of environmental exposures (e.g., PFAS) and health biomarkers (e.g., inflammatory factors) are crucial for establishing global trends and setting safety standards. However, the over-representation of clinical cohorts and non-community-based samples in research databases may systematically skew estimates, as these populations often have higher baseline risk or exposure levels. This bias, if unquantified, undermines the representativeness of pooled data and the validity of public health policies derived from them.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-analytic estimates of global per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance (PFAS) exposure levels and associated serum inflammatory factor levels are significantly inflated when derived from pooled datasets that include clinical or non-community-based samples, compared to estimates based solely on representative, community-based populations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and bibliometric analysis (using tools like VOSviewer or CiteSpace, pre-2026) to map the landscape of PFAS and inflammatory biomarker research, categorizing studies by population source (community-based, clinical, occupational). For key biomarkers (e.g., serum PFAS, IL-6, CRP), we will perform a comparative meta-analysis. Using random-effects models, we will calculate pooled summary estimates separately for community-based studies and for all studies combined. The difference between these estimates will be quantified as the 'representativeness bias index' (RBI). Subgroup and meta-regression analyses will test if study design and population source are significant moderators of effect size.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative measure of bias introduced by non-representative sampling in environmental epidemiology syntheses. It will yield a methodological framework for assessing data representativeness in pooled analyses and offer corrected, more generalizable baseline estimates for key PFAS and inflammatory markers to inform global health assessments.
proposals.novelty: While bibliometric reviews of specific fields (e.g., atherosclerosis, macrophage biology) exist, this study uniquely integrates bibliometric mapping with comparative meta-analysis to directly quantify sampling bias, applying it to the critical and timely intersection of pervasive environmental contaminants (PFAS) and systemic inflammation.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Drivers of Divergent Regional Blood Pressure Trends: A Multi-Domain Analysis of Dietary, Economic, and Health Policy Factors
proposals.motivation: Global hypertension prevalence shows significant regional disparities, with trends diverging despite widespread awareness. The specific, modifiable drivers behind these divergent patterns—particularly the interplay of dietary factors (e.g., sodium, PFAS exposure), economic development, and health policy implementation—remain inadequately quantified. Understanding these drivers is critical for designing targeted, region-specific public health interventions to curb the cardiovascular disease burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that regional divergence in blood pressure trends is primarily driven by specific, interacting clusters of dietary patterns (reflected in serum acid and inflammatory factor levels), economic inequality metrics, and the intensity of national hypertension treatment and salt-reduction policies.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method approach integrating bibliometric analysis and multivariate regression modeling. First, a bibliometric analysis (using tools like VOSviewer or CiteSpace) will map the existing research landscape on regional cardiovascular health determinants. Second, we will construct a panel dataset from pre-2026 sources (e.g., WHO NCD reports, FAO food balance sheets, World Bank indicators) to quantify regional exposures. A fixed-effects regression model will then be used to analyze the association between these factors (dietary, economic, policy) and regional blood pressure levels, testing for significant interactions between domains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a synthesized, evidence-based map of the key modifiable drivers of regional blood pressure disparities, moving beyond descriptive trends to causal inference. It will directly inform health policy by identifying which combinations of factors (e.g., economic stress coupled with high processed food intake) most strongly predict adverse outcomes, allowing for prioritized intervention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrative, multi-domain analytical framework that combines bibliometric trend visualization with rigorous statistical modeling of hard endpoints (blood pressure), specifically linking emerging dietary concerns (like PFAS) and treatment policy data to explain macro-level epidemiological patterns.

proposals.title: Integrating Global Blood Pressure Trend Estimates with Cardiovascular Disease Burden Projections: A Computational Modeling and Bibliometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Despite significant advances in cardiovascular disease (CVD) treatment, the global burden remains high. Pre-2026 studies, including bibliometric analyses of atherosclerosis and myocardial ischemia, highlight the importance of risk factor trends. However, there is a critical gap in quantitatively linking longitudinal population-level blood pressure (BP) trends, a key modifiable risk factor, to refined forecasts of future CVD burden, which is essential for proactive public health resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating high-resolution, spatiotemporal blood pressure trend estimates from global cohort data with established CVD risk models will generate more accurate and granular projections of future disease burden than current methods that rely on static risk factor assumptions.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage computational modeling approach. First, we will use hierarchical Bayesian models to estimate national and sub-national systolic and diastolic BP trends from 1990-2025 using data from the NCD-RisC consortium. Second, these trend estimates will be integrated into the cardiovascular component of the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) comparative risk assessment framework via a modified proportional multi-state life table model. Bibliometric analysis, as used in the researcher's prior work, will inform the selection of key mechanistic pathways (e.g., inflammation, atherosclerosis) to weight in the integration algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, open-access model linking BP trends to CVD burden forecasts at a sub-national level for key countries. It will provide a methodological framework for integrating other time-varying risk factors (e.g., PFAS exposure, lipid levels) into disease projections, directly supporting targeted prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dynamic coupling of continuous risk factor trend modeling with disease burden forecasting, moving beyond static snapshots, and the application of bibliometric insights to parameterize the biological plausibility of the trend-burden linkage within a computational fluid dynamics-inspired systems model.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Historical Population Sodium Intake from Long-Term Blood Pressure Trends: A Computational Modeling and Validation Study
proposals.motivation: Excessive dietary salt intake is a major modifiable risk factor for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, but long-term, population-level consumption data are scarce and unreliable. Reconstructing historical salt intake patterns is crucial for understanding the drivers of cardiovascular disease trends and evaluating the impact of past public health interventions. This study addresses this gap by developing a novel inverse modeling approach to infer past intake from widely available blood pressure data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observed secular trends in population mean systolic blood pressure (SBP) can be used to inversely estimate historical sodium intake patterns by leveraging established dose-response relationships within a validated physiological model.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the comparative risk assessment framework used in the Global Burden of Disease study, specifically the dose-response curve linking sodium intake to SBP. Using time-series data on population SBP (e.g., from NHANES cycles, NCD-RisC) as the observed output, we will formulate an inverse problem to solve for the latent sodium intake time series. The core algorithm will involve a Bayesian state-space model, where a Kalman filter or particle filter will be used for sequential estimation, incorporating prior distributions on intake from urinary sodium studies and accounting for confounding factors like BMI and potassium intake trends through multivariate adjustment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for reconstructing historical dietary exposures from health outcome data. It will generate the first long-term, model-based estimates of population sodium intake trends, offering new insights for retrospective policy analysis. The validated model can serve as a tool for other researchers to estimate intake in data-poor settings.
proposals.novelty: While forward models predicting BP from salt intake are common, inverting this relationship to robustly estimate intake from BP trends at a population level is a novel, non-trivial computational challenge. The approach uniquely combines epidemiological dose-response data with state-space estimation techniques from engineering to solve a public health history problem.

proposals.title: Optimizing Global Cardiovascular Health: A Population-Level Modeling Study to Determine the Blood Pressure Distribution Minimizing Total Disease Burden
proposals.motivation: Hypertension is the leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) globally, but optimal population-level blood pressure (BP) targets remain debated. Current guidelines focus on individual risk thresholds, not the shape of the entire population distribution. A systematic, model-based approach to define the BP distribution that minimizes total CVD burden (including myocardial infarction and stroke) could provide a powerful, evidence-based target for public health policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a specific, non-normal global population blood pressure distribution—characterized by its mean, variance, and skewness—that minimizes the total projected incidence of major cardiovascular events when modeled against established risk functions.
proposals.method: We will construct a microsimulation model integrating pre-2026 global demographic data (from sources like the NCD-RisC collaboration) and established CVD risk equations (e.g., WHO or Framingham-based). The model will treat the population BP distribution as a flexible parameterized function. Using a computational optimization framework (e.g., a genetic algorithm or Bayesian optimization), we will iteratively adjust the distribution's parameters to minimize the total model-projected CVD events, subject to constraints representing biological plausibility. This approach combines Jun Ma's expertise in bibliometric analysis of cardiovascular topics and biochemical level modeling.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, quantitative benchmark for the 'ideal' population blood pressure profile, moving beyond single-threshold guidelines. It will offer a rigorous, model-derived target for evaluating the potential impact of national and global hypertension control programs. The methodology will also establish a framework for optimizing other risk factor distributions (e.g., cholesterol) at the population level.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have identified optimal *individual* BP targets, this work uniquely seeks the optimal *population-level distribution*, a non-obvious public health target that accounts for the continuous relationship between BP and risk across an entire demographic. It applies computational optimization techniques common in engineering to a core epidemiological question.

ideas.title: Test if cortical morphology mediates population- and time-specific BMI-MACE relationships
ideas.description: This research investigates whether cortical morphology indices serve as a mediating neurobiological substrate that explains the population- and time-dependent variability in the relationship between BMI and major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE). It tests the hypothesis that differences in brain structure, which reflect cumulative life-course exposures and stress, modify the optimal BMI threshold for cardiovascular risk across different demographic groups and birth cohorts. By integrating neuroimaging with longitudinal health data, the study aims to develop prediction models where cortical morphology dynamically calibrates the BMI-MACE association, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all cutoff to a personalized, pathophysiology-aware risk assessment framework.

ideas.title: Cardiometabolic Dual Burden and Neurostructural Vulnerability: A Household-Level Life Course Investigation
ideas.description: This study investigates how the co-occurrence of underweight and obesity within the same household creates a shared cardiometabolic stress environment, potentially driving early, subclinical cardiac dysfunction in all members. It then assesses whether this household-level dysfunction, measured via advanced imaging and biomarkers, is associated with accelerated decline in cortical morphology and brain structure in adults, prior to overt heart failure. The research moves beyond treating established disease to identifying how familial nutritional disparities act as a preventable upstream driver of both cardiovascular and neurostructural decline, integrating social, cardiovascular, and neurological determinants of health across the life course.

ideas.title: Linking National Health Policy Language to Neuroanatomical Progression in Heart Failure
ideas.description: This study longitudinally tracks cortical morphology changes in heart failure (HF) patients while concurrently applying NLP to analyze the language of national health policies active during the study period. The core innovation is to correlate temporal shifts in policy focus (e.g., towards mental health integration, patient self-management, or specific treatment guidelines) with the rate and pattern of brain structural changes in the cohort. This approach tests the hypothesis that systemic healthcare priorities and frameworks, as encoded in policy documents, may influence clinical practice and patient management in ways that measurably affect neurological outcomes in HF over time.

ideas.title: Causal and Independent Contribution of Cortical Morphometry to MACE Risk: A Multi-Method Validation
ideas.description: This study employs a triangulation of methods to rigorously evaluate whether cortical morphology provides independent and causally relevant information for MACE prediction. It integrates longitudinal cohort data to establish temporal sequence, Mendelian Randomization to assess potential causality, and nested model testing within a comprehensive risk factor framework. The final predictive model is developed in a primary cohort and its specificity is validated in an independent cohort where the added value over gold-standard clinical risk scores and subclinical vascular disease markers is quantified. This approach distinguishes mere correlation from actionable, independent prognostic signal.

ideas.title: Causal inference study on the effect of guideline-directed heart failure therapy on cortical morphology using target trial emulation and instrumental variable analysis
ideas.description: This study employs a causal inference framework to isolate the effect of heart failure (HF) treatment on brain structure from confounding factors. Using a target trial emulation approach with longitudinal clinical and neuroimaging data, it compares cortical morphology trajectories between new users of different guideline-directed medical therapies, matching on a robust set of time-varying confounders. To address unmeasured confounding, the design incorporates an instrumental variable analysis based on regional prescribing practice variations. This method aims to provide a more rigorous, less biased estimate of whether intensification of HF therapy can causally alter the rate of brain structural decline.

ideas.title: Dynamic Brain-Heart Connectome Biomarkers for Personalized Cardiometabolic Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of dynamic BMI thresholds and brain-heart connectomics to investigate how individual variations in the central autonomic network's architecture and function determine personalized optimal ranges for body composition and metabolic health. Using network neuroscience methods in heart failure and at-risk populations, it will model the brain-heart connectome to identify neural circuit signatures that predict an individual's specific cardiometabolic risk trajectory relative to their BMI. The core hypothesis is that a dysregulated brain-heart network defines a 'neural phenotype' for which conventional static BMI cutoffs are invalid, necessitating person-specific thresholds. This synergy enables a shift from population-level epidemiological benchmarks to a precision medicine framework where risk is defined by the integrity of the individual's neurovisceral integration system.

ideas.title: Modeling Cortical Morphology Trajectories in Heart Failure Using Climate Change Prediction Frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts the ensemble modeling approach from climate science to study cortical morphology changes in heart failure (HF). In climate science, an ensemble of global circulation models is used to project future climate variables, separating robust trends from model-specific uncertainty. Adapted here, the study would track longitudinal MRI-derived cortical thickness and gyrification in HF patients but analyze the data using an ensemble of predictive computational models. This method would generate a range of possible future trajectories for each patient or subgroup, identifying which aspects of cortical decline are consistently predicted (and thus likely central to HF-related neurodegeneration) and which are highly variable (potentially modifiable by treatment or comorbidities). The output would be a set of validated, uncertainty-quantified progression models that could stratify patients by risk of rapid morphological decline.

ideas.title: A Multi-Parameter Composite Index for Dual Burden of Malnutrition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that BMI is a sufficient sole metric for defining the dual burden of underweight and obesity by incorporating additional measures like waist circumference and body fat percentage. The analogy is drawn from materials science and engineering, where the limitations of a single property metric (e.g., tensile strength) are overcome by using composite indices and multi-axial performance charts (like Ashby charts) to map materials for complex applications. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to develop a multi-parameter composite index or a 2D/3D 'health phenotype chart' for malnutrition. This tool would plot individuals within a space defined by axes such as BMI, waist circumference, and body fat percentage, creating distinct regions that more accurately identify and categorize at-risk phenotypes—such as those with normal weight but high visceral fat or underweight with adverse metabolic profiles—thereby refining public health screening and intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Leveraging Geotechnical Soil Stratigraphy Analysis to Integrate Cortical Morphology into Cardiovascular Risk Prediction
ideas.description: This research seed proposes an analogical approach inspired by geotechnical engineering's methods for predicting structural failures. In that field, analyzing subsurface soil stratigraphy—integrating diverse sensor data to model hidden weaknesses—provides a far superior prediction of collapse than surface inspection alone. We adapt this solution to cardiovascular risk prediction. The investigation will test whether detailed cortical morphology indices (e.g., cortical thickness, gyrification index, surface area), derived from structural MRI and analogous to geological stratigraphy, can improve prediction models for Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events (MACE). The adapted idea involves creating a multimodal risk model that integrates these 'neural stratigraphy' biomarkers with conventional risk factors. The core hypothesis is that the brain's cortical architecture encodes long-term vascular insult, providing a novel, integrative biomarker layer that enhances the identification of high-risk individuals beyond current clinical tools.

ideas.title: Pre-registered, Multi-Cohort Stratified Discovery and Validation for HF Subtypes
ideas.description: This study employs a pre-registered, two-phase design to rigorously identify and validate subtype-specific associations. In the discovery phase, a large, deeply phenotyped cohort is used to perform stratified analyses across pre-defined HF etiologies and severity bins, generating subtype-specific hypotheses. These hypotheses, along with their specific statistical thresholds, are then tested in one or more independent, pre-specified validation cohorts of comparable clinical granularity. The analysis plan explicitly accounts for multiple comparisons across all strata and outcomes to control the false discovery rate, ensuring that any validated findings are robust and not artifacts of cohort-specific bias or data dredging.

ideas.title: Stratified Enrichment and Biomarker Validation in a Multi-Etiology Heart Failure Cohort
ideas.description: This idea adapts the proven oncology strategy of validating broad findings through precise, biomarker-informed patient stratification. Instead of a simple replication in a larger cohort, the study will be designed as a prospective, multi-center validation cohort with an adaptive enrichment framework. It will pre-specify enrollment targets for heart failure patients stratified by rigorously adjudicated etiology (e.g., ischemic, genetic dilated cardiomyopathy, cardiac amyloidosis) and severity (using standardized imaging and functional class criteria). Centralized core lab analysis will ensure consistent subtyping, mirroring the role of centralized pathology in cancer trials. This approach will test whether the original findings are generalizable or, more importantly, are driven by specific, mechanistically defined subgroups, thereby refining the pathophysiological understanding and future targeted trial design in heart failure.

ideas.title: Bayesian Forecasting of Brain Health Gains from Heart Failure Policy Interventions
ideas.description: This research integrates longitudinal neuroimaging data on heart failure (HF) patients with a Bayesian forecasting framework to project the future combined prevalence of HF and associated cortical thinning under different healthcare policy scenarios. It models how scaling up specific, evidence-based HF treatment optimizations (e.g., guideline-directed medical therapy penetration rates) would probabilistically alter the trajectory of brain morphology decline at a population level over time. The output provides policymakers with quantifiable forecasts of the neuroprotective benefits of cardiovascular care investments, directly linking clinical intervention levers to long-term brain health outcomes.

ideas.title: Disentangling Hypoxic Fraction: A Causal Inference Model for Isolating Duration's Effect on Cortical Injury from Concurrent Physiological Insults
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple correlation to investigate whether HF duration has an independent causal role in brain damage, distinct from the severity of accompanying physiological insults. We implement a prospective observational design with continuous multimodal monitoring to capture the full hypoxic episode's physiology. By applying causal inference statistical methods, we model the treatment effect of duration while controlling for confounders like hypoxia depth, hypotension, and patient premorbid status. The outcome is a quantified, adjusted estimate of duration's specific contribution to injury, revealing if its relationship is linear, threshold-based, or negligible when other factors are accounted for.

ideas.title: Adapting Pediatric Pharmacology's Developmental Z-Score Methods for Early Childhood BMI Trend Analysis
ideas.description: The original idea to include pre-school children in global BMI trend analysis faces the challenge of their rapid, non-linear growth, making standard adult BMI categories invalid. Drawing an analogy from pediatric pharmacology, which solves the analogous problem of including children in drug studies by using age- and sex-specific Z-scores based on reference growth standards, we adapt this solution. The research idea is to apply the WHO Child Growth Standards to calculate BMI-for-age Z-scores for children under five in existing and future global health surveys. This adapted approach creates a developmentally appropriate, normalized metric, enabling the valid tracking and comparison of underweight and overweight trends in this young age group across countries and over time, thus fully integrating them into global public health analyses.

ideas.title: Network diffusion of cortical thinning as a consequence of cognitive decline in heart failure
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network science diffusion models with the causal question of cortical thinning in heart failure (HF). It posits that cognitive decline in HF originates from a non-structural, metabolic or vascular insult, which then triggers a secondary, network-dependent spread of cortical thinning across structurally connected brain regions. By modeling the brain as a structural connectome, the project will test if the observed spatial pattern of atrophy is best predicted by a diffusion process on this network, seeded at regions of initial functional impairment. This approach directly tests the 'consequence, not cause' hypothesis by examining the dynamic, network-driven progression of atrophy itself.

ideas.title: High-resolution subnetwork analysis of the brain-heart connectome in heart failure
ideas.description: This research direction applies the principle of high-resolution, subnational analysis to the connectomic study of the brain-heart axis in heart failure. Instead of modeling the brain as a monolithic or broadly parcellated network, it employs network neuroscience methods to construct and analyze the connectome at the resolution of distinct cortical, subcortical, and brainstem circuits. The core innovation is to identify which specific, high-resolution neural subnetworks—such as those governing autonomic control, baroreception, or interoception—exhibit the most aberrant connectivity patterns and to correlate these precise circuit dysfunctions with granular clinical and physiological outcomes in heart failure patients. This approach aims to discover targeted neural biomarkers for disease subtypes and progression, offering a more mechanistic foundation for neuromodulatory therapies.

By correlating these linguistic patterns with national health survey data, the study aims to determine if and how the evolution of policy discourse precedes or coincides with measurable changes in the prevalence of the dual burden of malnutrition when defined by these more accurate metrics. This creates a novel evidence base for how policy wording might directly influence the conceptualization and measurement of a major public health challenge.

ideas.description: This research fuses duration-damage correlation with connectomics to model how the brain-heart network degrades over time in heart failure (HF). It will test the hypothesis that HF duration correlates with cortical damage in a non-linear, threshold-based manner, specifically by identifying critical points where key network hubs or connections fail. By applying network neuroscience methods to longitudinal multimodal data (e.g., fMRI, cardiac output), the study aims to construct dynamic connectome models that can predict the temporal tipping point for accelerated neural deterioration. This provides a mechanistic framework for timing interventions to preserve network integrity before irreversible damage occurs.

ideas.title: Apply Causal Forest Algorithms from Biostatistics to Evaluate Nutrition Policies
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from biostatistics—specifically, the use of causal forest algorithms for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects in clinical research—to the evaluation of nutrition policies. The analogous problem in biostatistics involves determining how a medical treatment's effectiveness varies across different patient characteristics in complex, non-randomized data. By applying causal forests to observational data on nutrition policy implementation (e.g., from health surveys or sales data), researchers can move beyond average treatment effects from methods like difference-in-differences. The adapted research would identify heterogeneous policy impacts, revealing which population subgroups (defined by income, education, region, or baseline health) benefited most or least from the intervention, thereby generating actionable insights for equitable and effective policy scaling.

ideas.title: Global nutritional status as a developmental driver of cortical maturation and cognitive trajectory in youth
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that the global dual burden of malnutrition (combined underweight and obesity) in school-aged children and adolescents is a primary environmental driver of atypical cortical maturation, which in turn mediates cognitive and academic impairment. It moves beyond correlation by using longitudinal cohort data from diverse global settings to test if nutritional status predicts the rate and pattern of cortical thinning/thickening in key cognitive regions, and whether these structural changes statistically mediate the relationship between nutrition and later cognitive performance. The synergy lies in reframing cortical thinning not just as a biomarker of decline, but as a modifiable developmental pathway through which population-level nutritional trends directly shape global brain health and human capital outcomes.

ideas.title: Applying Composite Climate Indices to Model Dual Burden of Malnutrition Health Impacts
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from climate science's use of composite indices like the SPEI to model complex environmental stressors, this research adapts the solution to public health. We propose creating a standardized, national-level Dual Burden of Malnutrition (DBM) Index that combines time-series data for underweight and obesity prevalence into a single metric. This composite index will then be used as the key independent variable in econometric and epidemiological models to correlate its fluctuations with specific health outcome data (e.g., cause-specific mortality, disability-adjusted life years), controlling for confounders. This approach moves beyond simple correlation of combined prevalence to model the DBM as a unified population-level risk factor, analogous to how a drought index predicts agricultural yield losses.

ideas.description: This research moves beyond simply adding variables to the dual-burden framework by employing a person-centered, computational typology approach. Using multi-source national survey data, it applies clustering techniques to identify sub-populations with distinct, coherent profiles across the full spectrum of anthropometry, micronutrient status, and diet quality indicators. The system then uses feature importance analysis to pinpoint the key demographic, economic, and food environment drivers unique to each malnutrition profile. The output is a practical map of population subtypes, such as 'micronutrient-deficient with adequate energy' or 'poor diet quality with early obesity risk,' enabling precisely targeted policy and program design.

ideas.title: Causal Mechanistic Bridging: Testing if Functional Dynamics Mediate Structural-Impairment Predictions
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond a simple sufficiency challenge to actively test causal mechanistic pathways. It begins by establishing the predictive power of structural MRI for a clinical impairment. Then, within an experimental or longitudinal observational design, it tests whether interventions that alter brain function or metabolism subsequently modify the impairment in a manner predicted by the structural model. The core advance is a formal mediation analysis that quantifies whether functional/metabolic changes are necessary to explain how structural predictors lead to behavioral outcomes, providing direct evidence for or against a purely structural mechanism.

ideas.title: A Unified Malnutrition Index via a Composite Risk Metric
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from materials science, where diverse defects are combined into a single damage index. The analogous problem is unifying different types of material flaws (cracks, voids) into one metric weighted by their contribution to failure. The adapted research idea is to develop a Unified Malnutrition Index for populations. This index would combine the prevalences of underweight and obesity, weighting each by its relative population health burden (e.g., using established risk data for mortality or morbidity). The result is a single, composite metric that measures the total burden of malnutrition from both nutritional deficits and excesses, challenging the assumption that these must be tracked separately.

ideas.title: Adapting Dynamic Threshold Models from Species Conservation to BMI Cutoffs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of static BMI cutoff validity by drawing an analogy from conservation biology, where static thresholds for species endangerment have been replaced by dynamic, context-dependent models like population viability analysis. The analogous solution integrates species-specific traits and environmental trends to set adaptable risk thresholds. Adapted to BMI research, this involves developing a dynamic modeling framework that uses large-scale longitudinal health data to calculate BMI thresholds for health risks (e.g., diabetes, CVD) as a function of population-specific covariates (e.g., ethnicity, age, sex, socioeconomic status, dietary patterns, and physical activity trends). The research would investigate how these optimal thresholds vary across different sub-populations and shift over time in response to changing environmental and lifestyle factors, moving beyond a single, fixed cutoff to a more nuanced and personalized risk assessment tool.

ideas.title: Adapting Markov Chain Models from Population Ecology to Map BMI Category Transitions
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that analyzing static prevalence of BMI categories is sufficient. By drawing an analogy from population ecology, we adapt its solution for modeling population dynamics. Ecologists use stage-structured matrix models (like Lefkovitch matrices) to quantify probabilistic flows of individuals between life stages (e.g., seedling to sapling) over time, revealing the drivers of population structure change. We adapt this by defining BMI categories as 'stages' and using longitudinal health data to estimate a transition probability matrix. This model will quantify the annual likelihood of individuals moving between BMI categories, remaining stable, or entering/leaving the study population. The result is a dynamic systems view of obesity epidemiology that identifies the most frequent transition pathways, stability within categories, and the net flows driving changes in prevalence, moving beyond static snapshots to model the underlying individual-level movements.

ideas.title: Apply ecological network stability analysis to the brain-heart connectome in heart failure
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology, where network theory is used to model the stability and resilience of food webs to perturbations. The analogous solution involves constructing interaction networks, calculating metrics like modularity and connectivity, and identifying keystone species whose removal destabilizes the system. Adapted to the research context of heart failure, this involves using network neuroscience methods to model the brain-heart connectome not just structurally, but functionally as a dynamic, integrated system. By applying ecological stability analysis, we can treat neural centers, autonomic pathways, and cardiac outputs as interacting nodes and links. This allows for the identification of critical 'keystone' hubs within the brain-heart network and the quantification of the network's robustness, providing a predictive model for how the system loses stability and tips into states of acute decompensation in heart failure patients.

It moves beyond global cognitive scores to investigate how these early brain structure trajectories predict performance in distinct cognitive domains, such as executive function, language, and visuospatial processing. The approach enables the identification of potential neurodevelopmental pathways linking early childhood health trends to later, domain-specific cognitive outcomes. This synergy allows for public health metrics (BMI trends) to inform targeted neurocognitive risk stratification and early intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Challenging the sufficiency of structural MRI for mechanism by interrogating population representativeness in neuroimaging databases
ideas.description: This research direction critically examines whether structural MRI findings, often proposed as sufficient mechanistic explanations for neurological or psychiatric conditions, are robust and generalizable across diverse populations. It systematically analyzes major neuroimaging databases (e.g., UK Biobank, ADNI) to identify gaps in representation—such as by ethnicity, socioeconomic status, or global region—and tests whether purported structure-function relationships hold consistently within and across these subpopulations. The synergy lies in using demographic underrepresentation not just as a limitation to acknowledge, but as a methodological tool to stress-test mechanistic claims: if a structural biomarker's link to impairment disappears or radically changes in an underrepresented group, it challenges its validity as a universal mechanism and reveals a critical dependence on unmeasured contextual or biological factors.

ideas.title: Estimating the Economic Cost of a Population Health Transition via Thermodynamic Free Energy Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to calculating free energy changes in thermodynamic systems to estimate the economic impact of a population health transition from underweight to obesity prevalence. In the analogous field of statistical physics, the energy required to move a system between two equilibrium states is computed by modeling both enthalpic (internal energy) and entropic (disorder) components. The adapted idea applies this framework by defining the initial and final states as statistical distributions of BMI. The model quantifies an 'economic enthalpy' from direct, measurable costs (medical care, lost productivity) and an 'economic entropy' capturing the indirect costs of societal adaptation, behavioral friction, and the probability distribution of individual transitions within the population. By integrating these components across the shift, the model aims to produce a robust, scalable estimate of the total economic cost of this epidemiological transition, framing it as the 'work' needed to move the population between two metabolic equilibrium states.

ideas.title: Dynamic Co-Evolution Modeling of Socioeconomic Drivers and Population Bodyweight Distribution
ideas.description: This research develops a simulation framework to model the bidirectional causal pathways between a nation's economic structure and the distribution of bodyweight in its population. Instead of a simple cost-of-shift calculation, it models how factors like income inequality, food environment, and work-related physical activity feedback into shifting prevalence over time. The system can project the long-term economic and health outcomes of different policy or economic shocks, revealing leverage points for intervention. This provides a robust tool for understanding the systemic drivers of the obesity transition and its true economic ramifications.
