ideas.title: Causally-Validated Digital Twin ABM for Obesity Policy Prediction
ideas.description: This research constructs an agent-based model (ABM) of diet and physical activity where the micro-level behavioral rules are not assumed but are empirically derived and validated against high-frequency longitudinal data on individuals. The core innovation is to frame the ABM as a 'digital twin' system that must accurately retrodict the population-level BMI changes observed in response to real-world, quasi-experimental policy interventions, such as the implementation of a sugar-sweetened beverage tax or a new active transport infrastructure. Success in predicting these held-out causal impacts validates the model's mechanistic structure, moving it from a storytelling tool to a policy-testing platform. The final system can then be used to generate robust, comparative forecasts for novel policy bundles, with quantified uncertainty based on its validation performance.

ideas.title: A Life-Course Analysis of the Co-Occurrence of Multiple Forms of Malnutrition in Early Childhood
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a global trend analysis that maps the co-occurrence of stunting, wasting, overweight, and key micronutrient deficiencies (e.g., iron, vitamin A) among children under five. It integrates anthropometric data with biomarkers of diet quality and micronutrient status to create a multidimensional malnutrition profile for this age group. The core novelty is examining how these different forms of malnutrition manifest concurrently within populations, households, or as sequential conditions in the same child over time. This approach aims to identify common systemic drivers and critical periods for intervention that could simultaneously address multiple components of the malnutrition spectrum.

ideas.title: Dual Burden of Malnutrition Dashboard: A Disaggregated, Contextualized Monitoring System
ideas.description: This system develops a diagnostic dashboard that visualizes underweight and obesity as distinct, non-commensurate axes, plotting populations within this dual-burden space. It integrates contextual data on food security, inequality, and food environment to explain the observed burden distribution. The output is not a single score but a typology (e.g., 'high underweight, low obesity, high food insecurity') that directly informs specific, separable policy levers for each condition while highlighting shared systemic drivers.

ideas.title: Causal Evaluation of Nutrition Policies on the Global Double Burden of Malnutrition in Youth
ideas.description: This research applies quasi-experimental causal inference methods from economics—specifically difference-in-differences and synthetic control—to evaluate how specific nutrition policies (e.g., sugar taxes, school meal programs, food fortification) have affected the combined prevalence of underweight and obesity among school-aged children and adolescents globally. By constructing policy-treated units and matched control groups from international survey data (e.g., from UNICEF, WHO, or national surveys), it isolates the causal effect of these interventions on the double burden of malnutrition. This approach moves beyond documenting trends to empirically testing which policies simultaneously address undernutrition while not exacerbating—or even mitigating—the rise in childhood obesity, providing actionable evidence for integrated policy design.

ideas.description: This research seed is expanded via analogy to precision agriculture. In that field, continuous sensor data guides dynamic adjustments to irrigation/fertilization to optimize crop health. The adapted idea applies this solution to heart failure (HF) therapy for brain structure preservation. The study would assess if optimizing HF therapy using a precision medicine approach—with continuous multi-modal monitoring (e.g., hemodynamic sensors, cerebral oximetry) to dynamically personalize treatment targets—can more effectively halt or reverse cortical morphology decline compared to standard care. This frames the heart-brain axis as a system where real-time feedback loops, akin to agricultural control systems, are necessary for optimal 'structural yield' in the brain.

The approach moves beyond ecological correlation by establishing a direct, temporal link between environmental exposure and individual behavior, while controlling for spatial self-selection and confounding through matched control groups and individual-level covariates.

ideas.title: Structural MRI as a Sufficient Blueprint: A Materials Science Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that explaining cognitive or clinical impairment necessarily requires direct functional or metabolic imaging. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where static structure is used to predict dynamic properties via 'structure-property relationships,' we adapt this solution to neuroscience. The adapted research idea proposes that high-fidelity structural MRI (capturing connectome and morphometry) may be sufficient to infer mechanism if coupled with advanced computational models that simulate network dynamics and vulnerability. The core challenge shifts to developing and validating these neuro-structural-mechanistic models, testing if they can accurately predict functional impairments and clinical phenotypes without relying on concurrent fMRI or PET, thereby refining when additional imaging modalities are truly necessary.

It precisely measures nutritional status, dietary intake, and activity for each member, while mapping intra-household financial, food, and caregiving resources through surveys and time-use diaries. In-depth interviews explore social norms, gender roles, and decision-making that lead to unequal nutritional risks. This integrated approach identifies specific mechanisms—such as food allocation favoring wage-earners or gendered caregiving burdens—that explain the dual burden at the individual level within shared environments.

ideas.title: A Multi-Spectral Index for the Full Spectrum of Malnutrition
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from remote sensing to scale up dual burden analysis. In earth observation, complex landscapes are analyzed using multi-spectral indices that synthesize data from multiple light wavelengths into a single, informative metric (e.g., NDVI for plant health). Analogously, we propose developing a composite 'Malnutrition Spectrum Index' (MSI) for public health nutrition. This index would algorithmically integrate diverse data—such as stunting, obesity, anemia prevalence, and fruit/vegetable consumption—into a unified, spatially mappable score. This adapted approach moves beyond analyzing separate burdens, enabling a holistic, systems-view of how all forms of malnutrition (energy, micronutrient, diet quality) interact and co-exist within populations, which is critical for designing integrated food and health policies.

ideas.title: Geospatial Neurocognitive Morphology: Linking Cortical Structure to Cognitive Domains via Built Environment Exposures
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution geospatial data on urbanization and food environments with neuroimaging and cognitive testing to investigate how specific features of the built environment are associated with the morphology of cortical regions supporting distinct cognitive domains (e.g., prefrontal cortex and executive function, medial temporal lobe and memory). It moves beyond linking brain structure to cognition in isolation by embedding these relationships within their spatial and environmental context. The approach tests the hypothesis that environmental factors explain significant spatial variance in brain-cognition relationships, offering a new model for understanding the geographic disparities in cognitive health and aging.

ideas.title: Adapting Opinion Dynamics Models to Simulate Social Influence on Diet and BMI
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to modeling opinion formation from the field of sociophysics to the original problem of simulating BMI trends. The analogous solution uses agent-based models with bounded confidence rules to simulate how continuous opinions cluster in a population. This is adapted by treating an individual's propensity for healthy vs. unhealthy diet and activity choices as a 'health behavior tendency'—a continuous opinion-like variable. Agents are placed in a social network and update their tendency based on interactions with socially similar peers (using a bounded-confidence rule). This updated tendency then governs their individual choice rules within the simulation. This adaptation explicitly models the social contagion and clustering of behaviors, allowing the agent-based model to generate national BMI distribution trends that emerge from the complex interplay of individual psychology and social influence, moving beyond purely rational or isolated decision-making models.

ideas.title: Assessing the distributional economic burden of nutritional transition by quantifying bias from unrepresentative population data
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how the economic cost estimates of shifting from underweight to obesity prevalence are biased due to unrepresentative population health data. It moves beyond a single national cost figure to model how the economic burden is likely misallocated across different socioeconomic, ethnic, and geographic subpopulations because they are missing or underrepresented in surveys. The synergy creates a methodology to quantify the uncertainty and distributional injustice embedded in current economic models, showing which communities bear a hidden or exaggerated share of the projected costs. This directly informs more equitable data collection and targeted economic policy for nutrition transitions.

ideas.title: Projecting Combined Disease Burden Under Policy Scenarios Using Climate Model Ensemble Techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts the multi-model ensemble framework from climate science to forecast the dual burden of disease under policy scenarios. In climate science, ensembles of independent models are run under shared Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) to project future climate, quantifying uncertainty and robust trends. Similarly, we propose developing an ensemble of diverse epidemiological models (Bayesian, compartmental, agent-based) and running them under standardized, policy-driven intervention scenarios (e.g., varying levels of prevention, treatment, or economic measures). The output will be a distribution of future combined prevalence projections, providing a robust, uncertainty-aware forecast that identifies which policy scenarios most effectively alter the disease burden trajectory, moving beyond single-model dependence.

ideas.title: Geospatial-NLP Policy Impact Analysis for Urban Food Environments
ideas.description: This research integrates satellite-derived geospatial data on urban development and food retail environments with NLP analysis of the text and amendments of municipal and national health policies over time. By temporally aligning shifts in policy language (e.g., emphasis on 'food deserts,' 'zoning for fresh food,' or 'urban greening') with high-resolution spatial changes in the urban landscape, the study aims to identify which policy rhetorics and instruments are most strongly associated with on-the-ground improvements or deteriorations in food environment equity. This creates a novel evidence feedback loop, using observed geospatial outcomes to validate and refine the textual analysis of policy effectiveness.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismology's Precursor Signal Analysis to Policy Linguistics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from seismology, where scientists seek subtle precursor signals to forecast major events. The analogous problem is finding faint, predictive patterns within noisy data streams before a measurable change occurs. In seismology, this involves analyzing various signal types for correlative patterns with earthquakes. The adapted research idea applies this framework to policy analysis. National health policy documents are treated as a temporal sequence of textual signals. Using NLP, we extract specific linguistic features (semantic frames, sentiment trajectories, lexical choices related to urgency or commitment) as potential precursors. We then apply time-series correlation and pattern recognition methods, inspired by seismic precursor analysis, to test which language features systematically precede significant changes (e.g., in disease incidence, vaccination rates, or health equity metrics). The goal is to build a predictive model that identifies high-correlation 'policy linguistics precursors' to real-world trend shifts.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Assess and Correct for Population Representativeness Gaps
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a simple synthesis of available population studies yields unbiased national/global estimates by highlighting systematic gaps in the data for key subpopulations. Drawing an analogy from Ecology and Conservation Biology, where Species Distribution Models correct for spatial sampling bias, we propose a novel methodological adaptation. We will treat the set of included studies in a meta-analysis or systematic review as a biased sample within a multidimensional feature space defined by socio-demographic, economic, and geographic covariates. Using techniques analogous to ecological niche modeling (e.g., environmental matching, background sampling), we will map the 'representativeness niche' of the existing evidence, explicitly identify unsampled or undersampled combinations of population characteristics, and develop a quantitative framework to adjust aggregate estimates. This corrects for the non-random 'sampling' of subpopulations in the research literature, moving from a naive average of available data to an estimate corrected for representativeness gaps.

ideas.title: Household Nutritional Polarization: A Systems Ecology Approach
ideas.description: This research seed investigates the co-occurrence of underweight and obesity within the same families—the household-level dual burden of malnutrition. Drawing an analogy from Systems Ecology, which solves the puzzle of co-occurring species with opposite resource needs by analyzing micro-gradients and niche partitioning within a shared habitat, this study adapts that solution. It will conceptualize the household as a complex system and investigate the internal 'resource gradients'—such as inequitable food allocation, gendered eating patterns, sequential meal consumption, and preferential spending—that create distinct nutritional niches, allowing undernutrition and overnutrition to coexist under one roof. The goal is to move beyond documenting the phenomenon to modeling the specific socio-economic and behavioral mechanisms that drive this intra-household polarization.

ideas.title: Identify synergistic policy portfolios for dual burden reduction using a systems control framework
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecological restoration, where successful recovery of degraded systems hinges on managing synergistic 'keystone processes' rather than isolated interventions. The analogous solution involves identifying the minimal set of interlinked actions that create positive feedback loops to shift a system to a new stable state. Adapted to the original idea of studying countries that reduced both underweight and obesity, the research will model national nutrition as a dynamic system. It will identify and analyze the specific, synergistic portfolio of policies (e.g., combined fiscal, regulatory, and environmental actions) that functioned as a keystone process, enabling a coordinated reduction of both forms of malnutrition in those 11-17 successful countries.

ideas.title: Adapting Geologic Stratigraphy Mapping to Map Cortical Morphology-Cognition Links
ideas.description: This idea adapts the stratigraphic mapping approach from geology to neuroscience. In stratigraphy, precise correlation of rock layers across sites is achieved by identifying unique marker beds and fossil assemblages, not just overall layer thickness. Analogously, this research would seek to correlate specific, localized cortical morphology 'markers' (such as distinctive sulcal patterns or highly localized gyrification in predefined sub-regions of the prefrontal, temporal, or parietal cortex) with performance on finely dissected cognitive domain tasks. The goal is to move beyond global cognitive scores and generate a detailed, stratified map linking discrete morphological abnormalities to specific deficits in domains like executive function, memory subsystems, or language processing, thereby revealing more precise brain structure-cognition relationships.

ideas.title: Project future combined prevalence under dynamic, feedback-sensitive policy pathways
ideas.description: This research system forecasts dual burden trends by explicitly modeling the dynamic and adaptive nature of policy implementation and population response. It combines participatory simulation with stakeholders to generate ensembles of plausible, feedback-rich policy pathways, capturing unintended consequences and behavioral adaptation. These dynamic scenarios are formalized as probabilistic, time-varying inputs for a Bayesian forecasting model. The final output is a set of robust forecasts with quantified uncertainty, reflecting not just statistical error but also deep uncertainty about policy dynamics and system behavior.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Source-Tracking to Map Urban Food Environment Influences
ideas.description: This idea adapts the source-tracking methodology from epidemiology to the study of urban food environments. In epidemiology, genetic and spatial data are combined to model pathogen transmission from specific sources. Here, we propose using remote sensing (satellite/street view imagery) to identify and classify source features of the food environment (e.g., fast-food cluster hotspots, grocery store 'deserts'). These sources are then integrated with geospatial data on human movement (e.g., traffic flows, public transit routes) or simple network buffers in a spatial model. The adapted solution allows us to move beyond static mapping to dynamically model the spatial influence and 'reach' of different food environment types, explaining how specific urban forms might propagate population-level dietary health risks.

ideas.title: Nested Downscaling with Constrained Data Assimilation for Subnational Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the nested downscaling solution from Regional Climate Modeling (RCM) to enable high-resolution subnational analysis. In RCMs, a global model's outputs provide boundary conditions for a finer-scale regional model, ensuring consistency while resolving local detail. Similarly, we propose applying a national-scale modeling framework to the district or city level by using the national model outputs (e.g., resource allocations, policy impacts, aggregate trends) as dynamic constraints for a parallel, high-resolution subnational model. This 'nested' approach maintains coherence with top-down national data while allowing the bottom-up subnational model to reveal localized mechanisms, disparities, and system behaviors that are invisible at the country scale.

ideas.title: Multi-scale Geospatial Synthesis with Uncertainty-Quantified Aggregation for Subnational Analysis
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves the primary unit of analysis from arbitrary administrative districts to a standardized, fine-resolution global geospatial grid. A hierarchical Bayesian model is applied to this grid, integrating all available subnational data—including surveys, remote sensing, and crowd-sourced information—while explicitly accounting for measurement error and missingness. The model produces posterior estimates for all grid cells, from which estimates for any administrative unit (district, city) or functional region are derived via aggregation, always accompanied by principled uncertainty quantification. This ensures results are comparable across regions, robust to data sparsity, and transparent about their reliability.
